summary: Keep Photoshop CC updated via the Creative Cloud desktop app, then install Adobe Bridge to download photos from your camera and organize, preview, and open images. Change Photoshop’s default color settings to a working space like sRGB for better-looking photos, and sync those settings across Creative Cloud apps to maintain color accuracy. Adjust essential Preferences—such as interface customization, performance options, and auto-save intervals—to keep Photoshop running smoothly, and learn how to reset Preferences to defaults as a key troubleshooting step.

Set Photoshop as your default image editor in Windows 10 or Mac OS X, then open existing images using the File menu or drag-and-drop. Use Adobe Bridge to browse and select files, but fix file type associations in Bridge if images open in the wrong program. Open images directly into Camera Raw for raw processing, or move raw and JPEG files from Lightroom to Photoshop for further editing. Close all open images at once using the Close All command.

Navigate Photoshop's interface by exploring the toolbar, including how to reset or customize it in Photoshop CC. Manage panels, switch between tabbed documents and floating windows, and use multi-document layouts to view or move images between open files. Streamline your workspace with custom workspaces and maximize screen real estate with screen modes.

Navigate images in Photoshop using the Zoom Tool, Hand Tool, and basic navigation commands, then extend those skills to zoom and pan multiple images simultaneously. Use the Navigator panel to track your position when zoomed in, and enable Overscroll in Photoshop CC to scroll past image boundaries at any zoom level. Instantly jump between image areas with the hidden Birds Eye View feature, and rotate the canvas without rotating the image using the Rotate View Tool. The chapter concludes with essential tips, tricks, and keyboard shortcuts for efficient image navigation.

Resize images for print or web using the Image Size dialog box, controlling pixels, resolution, and file size. For pixel art, apply the Nearest Neighbor interpolation to keep edges crisp. Enlarge photos with Preserve Details 2.0 in Photoshop CC, which intelligently upscales while minimizing artifacts.

Crop images non-destructively using the Crop Tool, straighten tilted horizons, and add canvas space for borders. Master the Perspective Crop Tool to fix angled distortion, resize photos to exact frame sizes, and use Content-Aware Crop in Photoshop CC to intelligently fill empty areas. Crop images as circles by combining the Elliptical Marquee tool with a layer mask.

Select anything in an image using Photoshop’s full arsenal of selection tools. Draw geometric shapes with the Rectangular or Elliptical Marquee Tools, trace freehand edges with the Lasso, Polygonal Lasso, or Magnetic Lasso, or select by color using the Magic Wand Tool or the Color Range command. Paint over an object to select it instantly with the Quick Selection Tool, or trace surgically precise paths with the Pen Tool. The Focus Area command isolates only the in-focus parts of a photo. After making a selection, add to or subtract from it, or keep only the overlapping area. Use Free Transform to scale or rotate the selected content, or use Transform Selection to reshape the selection outline itself before committing.

Add a layer mask to any layer by clicking the Add Layer Mask icon at the bottom of the Layers panel. A white thumbnail appears linked to the layer; painting on that thumbnail with black hides parts of the layer, while painting with white reveals them again. Unlike lowering the Opacity slider, which fades the entire layer uniformly, or using the Eraser Tool, which permanently deletes pixels, a layer mask is non-destructive—you can switch between black and white brushes at any time to refine which areas are visible without losing any original image data.

Create a clipping mask by placing a content layer above a transparent layer; the bottom layer's content defines visibility for the layer above it, while transparent areas hide the clipped layer's content. Unlock the Background layer by clicking its lock icon, then Ctrl-click (Win) / Command-click (Mac) the Add New Layer icon to insert a blank layer below your image. Fill the bottom layer with a shape, gradient, or text using the Shape tool or Type tool, then right-click the top layer and choose Create Clipping Mask—or press Alt+Ctrl+G (Win) / Option+Command+G (Mac). The image now appears only where the bottom layer has pixels, fitting perfectly into shapes or text without altering the original layer.

Draw gradients across layers, selections, or layer masks using the Gradient Tool, selected from the Tools panel or by pressing G. Choose a preset gradient from the Gradient Picker by clicking the arrow next to the preview bar in the Options Bar, then double-click a thumbnail to apply it. Load additional themed gradient sets, such as Color Harmonies or Metals, from the gear icon menu in the Gradient Picker. Draw gradients by clicking and dragging across the canvas; the length and direction of the drag determine the gradient’s transition area and angle.

Convert a normal layer to a smart object in the Layers panel by right-clicking and choosing "Convert to Smart Object," then resize it freely with Edit > Free Transform or Ctrl+T (Win) / Command+T (Mac) without permanently discarding pixels. Unlike a standard pixel layer, which loses detail when scaled down because Photoshop throws away pixels, a smart object acts as a protective container that keeps the original image data intact. This means you can scale the smart object smaller and later scale it back up to its original size—or even larger—without any blockiness or blurriness, no matter how many times you resize it. Use the Layers panel to rename layers and set up a side-by-side comparison with a normal pixel layer to see the difference, and fill the Background layer with white via Edit > Fill for contrast.
Open the Brushes panel, select a brush preset, and enable six dynamic categories to control size, angle, color, and texture as you paint.

Open Photoshop's Home Screen by clicking the house icon in the upper left, then click the Profile button in the upper right corner to open your profile panel and view your current generative credits balance for the month, along with a Buy button for purchasing more. You cannot check your balance from the main workspace, so you must switch to the Home Screen first, and return to your document by clicking the PS logo.

Use the upgraded Select Subject or Remove Background commands in the latest Photoshop beta to make impossibly complex selections—like a bicycle with thin spokes—with a single click. First, ensure the feature runs on Adobe’s cloud servers by going to Edit (Win) or Photoshop (Mac) > Preferences > Image Processing and setting Select Subject and Remove Background to Cloud. With an image open, click Select Subject in the Contextual Task Bar (right-click the pasteboard to show it if hidden) to create a selection outline, or click Remove Background to instantly turn that selection into a layer mask that deletes the background. Both commands produce the same selection, but Remove Background automates the masking step.

Use the Frame Tool in Photoshop 2025 to place an image into any custom shape, including rectangles, ellipses, triangles, or custom shapes from the Shapes panel. Select the Frame Tool from the toolbar, choose a shape in the Options Bar, and drag out a frame on the canvas. The Layers panel shows a frame layer with separate thumbnails for the frame and its contents. Place an image by clicking the Contextual Task Bar’s Place button to browse your files, or use Generate image to create an AI-powered image via Adobe Firefly. The frame thumbnail must be selected before placing content; to reposition or scale the image inside the frame, click the contents thumbnail and use the transform handles.

Drag an image from Windows Explorer or Mac Finder onto Photoshop’s Home Screen or blank workspace to open it as a new document, or drop it directly into an existing document to add it as a new layer via Free Transform. To import multiple images as layers, select them all in your file manager and drag them into the document; Photoshop pauses after each import so you can resize or reposition with Free Transform, requiring you to click the check mark in the Options Bar to proceed through each image until all are added on individual layers.
Disable Photoshop's auto-show Home Screen in Preferences > General, then open and close a document to commit the change.

Cycle through Photoshop's screen modes by pressing F to hide interface elements progressively, or Shift+F to reverse direction. Full Screen Mode hides the toolbar, Options Bar, panels, menu bar, tab area, scroll bars, and status area completely, displaying only your image. Press Tab to toggle the toolbar, Options Bar, and panels on or off, or Shift+Tab to hide just the panels. Hold the spacebar to temporarily access the Hand Tool and reposition your image when interface elements are hidden. Access screen modes from View > Screen Mode, the toolbar's Screen Mode icon, or the F key.

Draw a live gradient by selecting the Gradient Tool (press G), setting Gradient Mode to Gradient in the Options Bar, and optionally choosing a preset from the Gradient Preset picker. Pick a style like Linear or Radial (cycle with bracket keys), leave Dither checked and Method on Perceptual, then click and drag on the canvas to create the gradient with a live preview. After drawing, use the on-canvas controls—color stops, midpoint sliders, and angle handles—to non-destructively edit colors, size, angle, and opacity, or save a custom preset from the Properties panel.

Crop an image into a perfect circle using the Elliptical Marquee Tool hidden behind the Rectangular Marquee Tool in the toolbar. First unlock the Background layer by clicking its lock icon so transparency is supported. Hold Shift while dragging to constrain the selection to a perfect circle, and add the spacebar to reposition the selection while still drawing. After the selection is placed, click the Add Layer Mask icon at the bottom of the Layers panel to instantly crop the image to the circle, leaving the corners transparent. Save the result as a PNG to preserve the transparency for use on any background.

Add a layer mask to a layer that already has a stroke effect, and the mask hides the stroke along with the image content, not just the image. To fix this, right-click the layer’s effects entry in the Layers panel, choose Create Layers to convert the stroke into its own separate layer, then apply the mask to that new stroke layer independently. For drop shadows, the mask also clips the shadow away; the solution is to place the shadow on its own layer by right-clicking the Drop Shadow effect and selecting Create Layer, then mask the shadow layer separately. Use the Select menu’s Transform Selection command to resize a selection outline, and the Add Layer Mask icon to apply masks.

Hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) and click the layer mask thumbnail in the Layers panel to switch the document view to the mask itself, then paste the copied image into that view using Edit > Paste. The image appears in the mask thumbnail, not as a separate layer. After pasting, use Image > Adjustments > Levels or Curves to boost contrast and control the mask’s effect intensity.
Use the new Brush Tip Outline option in Photoshop's Cursors Preferences to change the cursor thickness and stop losing it while painting.

Select the layer you want to crop, then use the Rectangular Marquee Tool to drag a selection around the area to keep. Resize the selection with Transform Selection from the Select menu, unlink the Width and Height fields in the Options Bar to unlock the aspect ratio, then invert the selection (Select > Inverse) and press Delete to crop away everything outside the selection. Remove the selection outline with Select > Deselect. This method is destructive and permanent, so for a flexible, non-destructive alternative use a layer mask instead.

Download and install the latest Photoshop beta through the Creative Cloud Desktop app by selecting Beta apps in the left column, locating Photoshop (Beta), and clicking Install. Both the official release and beta can run simultaneously, but the beta only opens from the Installed beta apps section, not All apps. Confirm you are running the beta via the Help > About Photoshop menu (Windows) or the Photoshop (Beta) label in the Menu Bar (Mac). Once open, click the Beta Feedback button in the upper right to see available preview features, report bugs, submit ideas, or join the beta community.

Open Photoshop’s Interface Preferences (Edit > Preferences > Interface on Windows, Photoshop > Preferences > Interface on Mac) and check the Neutral Color Mode option, introduced in the August 2022 release (version 23.5). This instantly replaces distracting static interface elements—like the blue Share button—with neutral gray, without affecting your image, the Color Picker, or the Gradient Editor. No restart is required; the change takes effect as soon as you click OK to close the dialog box.

Use the Object Selection, Quick Selection, or Magic Wand tool, then click the arrow next to the Select Subject button in the Options Bar to choose between Device (local processing for speed) and Cloud (Adobe’s servers for more detailed selections). Running Select Subject on the Cloud may produce more accurate results, especially with complex subjects like groups of people, but requires an internet connection. After comparing selections, set your preferred mode as the default in Photoshop’s Preferences under the new Select Subject category.

Turn off the Crop Tool’s Classic Mode in the Options Bar gear icon to keep images centered after cropping; using the Rectangular Marquee Tool with Image > Crop or the Crop Tool in Classic Mode always snaps the result to the upper-left corner, and even undoing a normal Crop Tool crop leaves the image off-center.

Resize a selection outline without affecting the pixels inside using Photoshop's Transform Selection command, found under the Select menu or by right-clicking inside an active selection. Drag any handle to scale the outline, hold Shift while dragging to unlock the aspect ratio, click and drag inside the box to reposition, or rotate by dragging just outside the box (hold Shift for 15-degree increments). Unlike Free Transform (Edit > Free Transform), which resizes the selected pixels, Transform Selection only alters the selection boundary itself.

Draw rectangular and elliptical selections using the Rectangular and Elliptical Marquee Tools, accessed from the toolbar or with the M keyboard shortcut (Shift+M toggles between them). Click and drag to create freeform rectangular or elliptical selection outlines, hold the spacebar while dragging to reposition the outline mid-draw, or click and drag inside an existing selection to move its outline without affecting pixels. Combine multiple selections by adding to or subtracting from them to build complex shapes, and use the marching ants boundary to isolate areas for editing.

Draw custom shapes in Photoshop using the Custom Shape Tool or the Shapes panel. Select the Custom Shape Tool from the toolbar, then open the Custom Shape Picker in the Options Bar to choose a shape from groups like Wild Animals or Leaf Trees. Set the Tool Mode to Shape, pick a fill color via the Fill swatch (solid, gradient, pattern, or custom from the Color Picker), then drag on the canvas to draw. Load hundreds of hidden shapes by clicking the gear icon in the Picker and choosing a library like Animals or Objects. Combine multiple shapes by drawing them on separate shape layers, then use the Merge Shape Components option in the Properties panel to unite them into one. Save any merged result as a new custom shape preset by selecting it and choosing Save Custom Shape from the Shapes panel menu.

Navigate the Photoshop toolbar by toggling between single and double column layouts using the double arrows at the top. Access hidden tools by clicking and holding or right-clicking any tool icon with a small arrow in its bottom right corner, then select from the fly-out menu. Each spot displays either the default tool or the last tool you selected, so you may need to reopen the menu to switch back. The toolbar logically groups related tools: Move and Selection tools at the top, followed by Crop and Slice, Measurement, Retouching and Painting, Drawing and Type, and finally Navigation tools at the bottom.

Draw shapes using the Rectangle, Ellipse, Triangle, Polygon, and Line tools, all nested in the toolbar behind the Rectangle Tool icon. Set the Tool Mode to Shape in the Options Bar to create scalable, editable vector shapes. Choose a Fill color from the swatch—select No Color, Solid Color, Gradient, or Pattern presets. Right-click the tool icon in the Options Bar and choose Reset Tool to restore default settings. Requires Photoshop 2022 or newer.

Press and hold the H key to temporarily select the Hand Tool, then click and hold on the image to zoom out to a full-screen bird’s-eye view. A rectangle representing your document window appears; drag it to any new area and release the mouse button to instantly zoom back in at the same magnification level. This hidden feature works from any tool and eliminates manual zooming and panning, letting you jump between image regions in a single fluid motion.

Hold the Shift key while dragging with the Hand Tool (or while holding the spacebar) to pan all open images simultaneously. To zoom all images at once, select the Zoom Tool, enable the Scrubby Zoom option in the Options Bar, then hold Shift and click-drag left or right on any image. Use Window > Arrange to choose a multi-document layout like 2-up Vertical so images are visible side-by-side; the active image’s tab appears brighter, and clicking any image or its tab switches focus. The Shift+Zoom and Shift+Pan tricks work only when multiple images are open and displayed in a multi-document layout.

Zoom in and out of images using the Zoom Tool, the Hand Tool, or the View menu’s Zoom In and Zoom Out commands, but speed up navigation with keyboard shortcuts: Ctrl++ (Win) / Command++ (Mac) to zoom in and Ctrl+- (Win) / Command+- (Mac) to zoom out, which jump to preset percentages. Check the current zoom level in the document tab or the lower-left corner, where you can double-click to type a custom value or hold Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) to activate the scrubby slider—drag right to zoom in, left to zoom out, and add Shift for 10% increments.

Add copyright and contact info to images using Photoshop’s File Info dialog box, then save the details as a reusable template. Open File > File Info, set the Basic category, enter your name in the Author field, change Copyright Status to Copyrighted, type a copyright symbol (Alt+0169 on Windows, Option+G on Mac) followed by your name, and add your website URL in the Copyright Info URL box. Copy that URL, switch to the IPTC category, and paste it into the Website(s) field under IPTC Contact. Save the template via the File Info dialog’s menu, but first delete the Document Title field to avoid overwriting it when applying the template to other images. Use Adobe Bridge to apply the template to multiple images at once.

Select people or objects instantly by enabling the Object Finder in the Options Bar after choosing the Object Selection Tool from the toolbar (nested with the Quick Selection and Magic Wand tools). Hover your cursor over any object in the image to see it highlighted, then click to create a selection without drawing any outline. For objects the Finder misses, fall back to the original method: set the Mode to Rectangle or Lasso, then drag a rough selection around the target; Photoshop analyzes that area and snaps the selection to the object. Add to an existing selection by holding Shift while dragging, and subtract by holding Alt (Win) / Option (Mac). The Object Finder works automatically in Photoshop 2022 and newer, but the manual drag method remains available for tricky subjects.

Right-click a JPEG (or any image type) in File Explorer, choose Properties, click the Change button next to “Opens with,” select Photoshop from the list (or use More apps or Look for another app on this PC to locate it), then click OK and close the Properties dialog. After that, double-clicking any JPEG will launch Photoshop and open the file directly. Repeat the same steps for PNG, TIFF, or other formats you want handled by Photoshop. If file extensions are hidden, enable them via View > Show > File name extensions first.

Draw a perfect 5-point star using the Polygon Tool set to 5 sides and 0 corner radius, then enable the Star Ratio option in the tool’s gear menu and set the ratio to 50% to align the inner points precisely. Hold Shift while dragging to keep the star upright, and release the mouse before Shift to finalize the shape. Save the star as a custom shape by going to Edit > Define Custom Shape, giving it a name, and accessing it anytime from the Custom Shape Tool’s shape picker. This method works in Photoshop 2021 or later, where the Polygon Tool’s Star Ratio control ensures perfectly symmetrical points.

Open an image with File > Open to place it in its own separate document, or use File > Place Embedded to insert an image as a new layer inside an already-open document. Place Embedded is grayed out until at least one document is open because it requires an existing target file. Using Open for two images creates two independent documents, each with its own tab and Layers panel, making it impossible to blend them directly. Place Embedded adds the image as a Smart Object layer, enabling immediate blending with layers in the active document.

Right-click an image file in Windows File Explorer, choose Properties, click the Change button next to “Opens with,” and select Photoshop from the list (click More Apps if it is not visible). On a Mac, Control-click the file, select Get Info, expand the “Open with” section, choose Photoshop from the dropdown, and click Change All to apply the setting to all files of that type. Repeat these steps for each file extension—JPEG, PNG, TIFF—you want Photoshop to handle by default.

Open the Gradients, Patterns, or Shapes panel, click the panel menu icon in the top right corner, and choose Legacy Gradients, Legacy Patterns and Textures, or Legacy Shapes and More from the fly-out list to load the classic presets hidden since Photoshop 2020. For hundreds of additional new shapes, open the Shapes panel menu and select Nature, Animals, Flowers, or another category to append those sets. Hold Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) while clicking a group’s arrow to expand or collapse all groups at once in any of these panels.

Crop images non-destructively using the Crop Tool (C) in Photoshop 2021. Select the tool, then drag the corner or edge handles to define the crop area; press Enter to apply. To straighten a crooked horizon, click the Straighten icon in the Options bar, then drag a line along something that should be horizontal or vertical. The key caveat: enable "Delete Cropped Pixels" in the Options bar to discard pixels permanently, or leave it unchecked to keep the full image hidden outside the canvas, allowing later repositioning with the Crop Tool.

Open the Creative Cloud desktop app from the system tray (Windows) or Menu Bar (Mac), or from Photoshop via Help > Updates. In the app, select the Updates category, then click the Update button next to Photoshop—close Photoshop first if prompted. After the update, click Open and verify the version number under Help > About Photoshop (Windows) or Photoshop > About Photoshop (Mac). To avoid losing custom settings when updating to a new major version, the tutorial explains how to keep the previous version installed and how to enable automatic updates in the Creative Cloud app’s preferences.

Select a Background, pixel, or type layer in the Layers panel and the enhanced Properties panel in Photoshop CC 2020 puts a wealth of controls at your fingertips. For the Background layer, you can now change image size, crop, trim, rotate the canvas, or apply Select Subject and Remove Background directly from the panel’s new Quick Actions. Pixel layers gain similar Quick Actions, while type layers display all Character and Paragraph panel options for instant text formatting, plus Quick Actions to convert type into a frame or shape. Open the panel via Window > Properties; twirl any group (Canvas, Rulers & Grids, Guides, Quick Actions) open or closed by clicking its arrow.

Remove backgrounds automatically using Photoshop CC 2020’s Select Subject or Remove Background commands, both found in the Properties panel after converting the Background layer to a standard pixel layer. Select Subject draws a selection and leaves it for you to refine or delete, while Remove Background immediately deletes the background. For better control, use Select Subject, then fix problem areas with the Object Selection Tool in the Select and Mask workspace.

Use Select Subject from the Select menu, the Options Bar with the Object Selection, Quick Selection, or Magic Wand tools active, or the Properties panel to automatically detect and select the main subject—people, animals, or objects—with one click, powered by Adobe Sensei AI. While the command often gets 90–95% of the selection correct, especially in CC 2020 and later, expect to refine edges in the Select and Mask workspace for tricky areas like hair or soft focus boundaries.

Open multiple images as layers in one step using File > Scripts > Load Files into Stack, which creates a new document and names each layer after the source file. For adding a single image into an existing document, use File > Place Embedded instead of Place Linked. Avoid checking Attempt to Automatically Align Source Images or Create Smart Object after Loading Layers unless you specifically need those behaviors. Toggle layer visibility with the eye icons in the Layers panel.

Open the Gradients panel (Window > Gradients) and create a new group via the panel menu's "New Gradient Group" command. Select the Gradient Tool, click the gradient swatch in the Options Bar to open the Gradient Editor, and choose the "Black, White" preset from the Basics folder. Change the black color stop to red (R:255, G:0, B:0) at 0% location, then click in a blank area of the preview bar to add a new color stop and set it to yellow. Continue adding stops for green, cyan, blue, and magenta, adjusting each stop's location to space the colors evenly across the gradient. Save your rainbow as a new preset by clicking "New" in the Gradient Editor, and it will appear in your custom group for applying to images, shapes, or text.
Scale images, type, and shapes proportionally by default with Free Transform in Photoshop CC 2020, using Shift to unlock the aspect ratio.

Scale, rotate, flip, skew, distort, and apply perspective to any unlocked pixel, type, shape, or smart object layer using Edit > Free Transform (Ctrl+T / Cmd+T). Drag corner or side handles to scale; hold Shift to constrain proportions. Rotate by moving the cursor just outside a corner handle until the curved arrow appears, then drag. Right-click inside the transform box to switch between Skew, Distort, Perspective, and Warp modes—Skew lets you slant the image by dragging a side handle, while Distort allows free movement of any corner handle independently. The Background layer is locked and cannot be transformed; unlock it by clicking the lock icon in the Layers panel. To avoid transparency when scaling smaller, add a Solid Color fill layer below the image via the New Fill or Adjustment Layer icon. Press Esc to cancel a transform without applying changes.

Use the Lasso Tool to draw a rough selection around an unwanted object, leaving a small margin of surrounding detail, then open Edit > Content-Aware Fill to enter the dedicated workspace introduced in CC 2019 and improved in CC 2020. The workspace splits into a work area on the left and a Preview panel on the right; the green overlay shows the sampling area that Photoshop can borrow pixels from. Adjust this sampling area with the Sampling Brush Tool to include or exclude specific regions, and use the toolbar’s other tools to rotate, scale, or mirror the sampled content for better blending. The Preview panel updates in real time, and you can resize it by dragging its left edge, then apply the fill non-destructively so the original image remains untouched.

Draw custom shapes in Photoshop using the Custom Shape Tool or the Shapes panel. Select the Custom Shape Tool from the toolbar, then open the Custom Shape Picker in the Options Bar to choose a shape from groups like Wild Animals or Leaf Trees. Set the Tool Mode to Shape, then pick a fill color—solid, gradient, pattern, or a custom color via the Color Picker. Most of Photoshop’s hundreds of shapes are hidden by default; load them from the Shapes panel’s fly-out menu. Combine and merge multiple shapes into one, then save the result as a new custom shape preset.

Drag a gradient thumbnail from the new Gradients panel directly onto a Background, pixel, shape, or type layer to apply it instantly in Photoshop CC 2020. Dropping onto a Background layer creates a Gradient fill layer above it, which you can then blend with the image by changing the fill layer’s blend mode (e.g., to Color) in the Layers panel. To swap gradients, select the Gradient fill layer and click a different thumbnail in the Gradients panel.

Open the Gradients panel from the Window menu, then click the panel menu icon and choose Legacy Gradients to restore the classic gradient sets from earlier Photoshop versions alongside the new default sets organized by color theme (Blues, Purples, Reds, and so on). Twirl any folder open or closed by clicking its arrow, or Ctrl-click (Win) / Command-click (Mac) any arrow to expand or collapse all sets at once. Change thumbnail size via the panel menu’s Small Thumbnail, Large Thumbnail, Small List, or Large List options. The top row shows recently used gradients, which you can hide by unchecking Show Recents in the same menu. Use the Create New Group, Create New Gradient, and Delete Gradient icons at the panel’s bottom to manage custom gradients, then edit them in the Gradient Editor.

Open an image in Photoshop CC 2020, duplicate the Background layer, then apply Filter > Pixelate > Mosaic to reduce the image to colored squares, adjusting the Cell Size slider (e.g., 80) to simplify the palette. In the Swatches panel, click the Create New Group icon to make a new folder for your custom set, then sample colors directly from the pixelated image using the Eyedropper tool and click the New Swatch button in the panel to add each color. Save your set as a custom .aco file via the panel menu to reuse later.

Drag colors directly from the improved Swatches panel in Photoshop CC 2020 onto any layer in your document. The panel now groups swatches into collapsible sets (RGB, CMYK, Pastels, etc.), which you can open or close all at once by Ctrl/Command-clicking any folder arrow. To apply a color, simply drag a swatch onto a pixel layer to fill its contents, onto a shape or type layer to change its fill, or onto the canvas to create a new Solid Color fill layer. For a Color Overlay layer effect, hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) while dragging. Access the panel via Window > Swatches, and adjust thumbnail size from the panel menu.

Save a Photoshop file as a .psdc (Photoshop Document Cloud) to store it online, then reopen and continue editing it on any PC, Mac, or iPad where you are signed into the same Creative Cloud account. View all your cloud documents from the Home Screen by selecting “Cloud documents” from the left menu, or open them via File > Open and clicking the “Open cloud documents” button. A small cloud icon on a thumbnail means the file has not yet been downloaded to your current device, so the first open will take longer while it caches locally. Rename, delete, or restore a deleted cloud document from the Home Screen.

Use the enhanced Warp command in Photoshop CC 2020 to bend and reshape images with far more precision than before. Access it via Edit > Transform > Warp or by clicking the Warp icon in the Free Transform Options Bar. The new Warp grid presets and custom grid sizes let you add your own grid lines and control points, select and warp multiple points simultaneously, and even scale or rotate different areas independently. Convert your layer to a smart object first (Layers panel menu > Convert to Smart Object) so the warp remains editable and reversible without quality loss.

Turn any active brush into an eraser by switching the Brush Tool’s blend mode from Normal to Clear—this instantly erases pixels on the current layer using the exact same brush tip, size, and settings you painted with. In Photoshop 2020 and later, an even faster method is to hold the tilde key (~) while painting with the Brush Tool; releasing the key returns to normal painting. Both techniques only work when painting on a separate layer above the background, because the Clear blend mode and tilde shortcut have no effect on the locked Background layer.

Select the Object Selection Tool from the toolbar (keyboard shortcut W) nested with the Quick Selection and Magic Wand tools. Before drawing, set the Mode to Rectangle or Lasso in the Options Bar; with Lasso mode, hold Alt/Option to temporarily switch to the Polygonal Lasso for clicking around objects. Drag a rough selection around your subject, and Photoshop automatically shrink-wraps the selection to its edges. To refine, drag around missed areas while holding Shift to add or Alt/Option to subtract, with Object Subtract enabling advanced subtraction technology.

Select the Custom Shape Tool from the Tools panel by clicking and holding the Rectangle Tool, then set the Tool Mode to Shape in the Options Bar. Click the shape thumbnail to open the Custom Shape Picker, then click the gear icon and choose All from the menu to load every shape set at once; click Append to add them to the existing defaults rather than replacing them. Draw any custom shape—hearts, animals, music notes, or copyright symbols—as a resolution-independent vector that stays crisp at any size.

Add layer effects and layer styles in Photoshop CC 2020 using the fx icon at the bottom of the Layers panel or the Layer > Layer Style menu, choosing from ten non-destructive effects like Drop Shadow, Stroke, and Outer Glow. A layer style is a collection of two or more effects plus blending options, while a single effect is just a layer effect. Effects cannot be applied to the Background layer because it lacks transparency, so ensure the target layer is a type, pixel, or shape layer. Use the new CC 2020 layer style presets for one-click results, and find missing older presets by loading them from the Styles panel menu.

Press B to select the Brush Tool instantly, then enable a crosshair in the brush cursor via Edit > Preferences > Cursors (Windows) or Photoshop CC menu > Preferences > Cursors (Mac) by checking “Show Crosshair in Brush Tip.” For smoother strokes, open Window > Brush Settings (or Brush panel in CS6), lower the Spacing value from the default 25% to 10% to eliminate bumpy edges caused by spaced dots. Change brush size faster using the left and right bracket keys ([ and ]) instead of right-clicking for the Brush Preset Picker slider.

Open images in Photoshop using the Home Screen, which appears when no documents are open and displays thumbnails of recent files for quick reopening. Click the Open button on the Home Screen to launch File Explorer or Finder, then double-click a JPEG or other image to open it. Return to the Home Screen at any time by clicking the Home button in the upper-left interface corner, then click Open again to add a second image. Close an image via File > Close. Note that opening a raw file differs from opening a standard JPEG and requires separate handling.

Create a new blank canvas in Photoshop using the New Document dialog box, accessed from the Home Screen’s “Create New…” button, the File > New menu, or the Ctrl+N/Command+N shortcut. Choose a preset size from categories like Photo, Print, Web, or Mobile, or enter custom Width, Height, Resolution, and Color Mode values in the panel on the right. Save your custom settings as a reusable preset using the “Save Preset” button, and toggle between the default and the older, smaller dialog box via the “Preset Details” icon for a more streamlined workflow.

Save the original image as a copy immediately after opening it by going to File > Save As, adding "_copy" to the filename, and clicking Save; this ensures all subsequent edits and saves overwrite only the copy, not the original. For JPEGs, choose Maximum quality and Baseline Optimized in the JPEG Options dialog. To work non-destructively, use layers, adjustment layers, or Smart Objects instead of direct edits like Image > Adjustments > Desaturate, which permanently alters pixel data and makes restoring the original impossible once the file is saved and closed.

Open Photoshop’s Preferences (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) and in the General category enable the **Use Legacy Free Transform** checkbox. This restores the pre-CC 2019 behavior where dragging any handle scales non‑proportionally by default; hold **Shift** while dragging a corner handle to scale proportionally. The option takes effect immediately—no restart required—and works in Photoshop CC 20.0.5 or later.

Press Shift+Ctrl+Alt+E (Win) or Shift+Command+Option+E (Mac) to merge a copy of all visible layers onto a brand new layer above the topmost layer, leaving the originals untouched. First select the top layer in the Layers panel so the merged copy appears above everything. Rename the resulting layer descriptively by double-clicking its name. This keyboard shortcut replaces the destructive Flatten Image command (Layer > Flatten Image) and preserves every layer for future editing.

Copy a single layer effect by holding Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) and dragging the effect name from one layer to another in the Layers panel. To copy all layer effects at once, Alt-drag the word "Effects" instead. For an exact visual match, copy the entire layer style—which includes blending options—by right-clicking the source layer, choosing "Copy Layer Style," then right-clicking the target layer and selecting "Paste Layer Style." You can also paste onto a layer group to apply effects to multiple layers simultaneously.

Enable faster layer selection by activating the Move Tool (press V) and checking Auto-Select in the Options Bar, then click any layer's content in the document to instantly highlight it in the Layers panel. To select multiple layers, drag a selection marquee around their contents with the Move Tool, or hold Ctrl (Windows) or Command (Mac) and click each item. Switch Auto-Select from Layer to Group in the Options Bar to select an entire layer group by clicking any layer within it. Clicking the Background layer's content deselects all layers, as the Background cannot be auto-selected.

Press and hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) and click the layer mask thumbnail in the Layers panel to display the mask as a grayscale image in the document window, making it easier to edit fine details. Use the same shortcut to toggle back to the composite view. To temporarily hide the mask’s effect without deleting it, Shift-click the mask thumbnail to place a red X over it; Shift-click again to re-enable the mask. Copy a mask from one layer to another by Alt-dragging (Win) / Option-dragging (Mac) the mask thumbnail onto the target layer. Feather a mask’s edges by double-clicking the mask thumbnail and adjusting the Feather slider in the Properties panel, or use the Masks panel’s Density slider to control overall opacity.

Use Paint Symmetry in Photoshop CC 2019 to create mirrored, symmetrical artwork by painting multiple brush strokes at once with the Brush, Pencil, or Eraser Tool. Click the butterfly icon in the Options Bar to open the Paint Symmetry menu and choose from ten options, including Vertical, Horizontal, Dual Axis, Radial, and Mandala. A blue symmetry path appears on the canvas, but note that it is for visual reference only and always affects the entire canvas regardless of its size, so resizing it is unnecessary. After selecting a symmetry option, paint on the canvas to see strokes mirrored automatically, and combine this feature with layer masks for more creative effects.

Select the Type Tool and click in your document to instantly see a live preview of your current font and type size using placeholder “Lorem Ipsum” text. Browse fonts or adjust the size with the scrubby slider in the Options Bar, and the preview updates in real time before you type your own text. Commit text by simply clicking away from it, no longer needing the Options Bar checkmark. Free Transform now scales type proportionally by default—hold Shift to switch to freeform mode, or hold Alt/Option to scale from the center.

Drag out a rectangular or elliptical frame with the Frame Tool (keyboard shortcut K), then use File > Place Embedded to drop an image into that frame. Hold Shift while dragging to force a square or circle, and press the spacebar mid-drag to reposition the frame. The image lands as a smart object inside a dedicated Frame layer, letting you resize or move the content independently. To swap images, simply drag a new one from your computer onto the frame. A stroke can be added around the frame from the Options Bar, but note that clipping masks offer more flexibility for advanced users.

Hover your cursor over any blend mode in the Layers panel’s Blend Mode menu to see a live preview of its effect on your image before committing. In Photoshop CC 2019, simply click the Blend Mode option to open the list, then mouse over each mode—Darken, Multiply, Screen, Overlay, and all 27 options—and the document instantly updates to show the result. No need to select or cycle with Shift+Plus/Minus; the preview disappears when you move the cursor away, letting you experiment risk-free.

Enable Content-Aware Crop in the Options Bar after selecting the Crop Tool, then use the Straighten Tool to drag a line across a horizon or edge; Photoshop rotates the image and automatically fills the resulting empty corners with matching detail instead of leaving checkerboard transparency. Extend the crop border beyond the original photo edges to add more room around subjects, and press Enter to apply—the tool generates plausible content in the expanded areas, though results vary depending on image complexity and surrounding detail.

Crop and resize an image to fit any frame size by using the Crop Tool with the "W x H x Resolution" preset from the Aspect Ratio menu in the Options Bar. Enter the exact frame dimensions (like 10 in by 8 in) and a resolution (300 ppi) into the Width, Height, and Resolution fields, then drag the crop border to compose the shot and press Enter. This simultaneously changes the aspect ratio and sets the print size, avoiding the distortion that occurs when unlinking Width and Height in the Image Size dialog box.

Select the Perspective Crop Tool from behind the standard Crop Tool in the Toolbar, then click and drag to draw a crop border around the image. Drag the corner handles to align the perspective grid lines with edges in the photo that should be vertical, such as the sides of a building, holding Shift to constrain movement horizontally. Press Enter to apply the crop, which simultaneously straightens the distorted edges and removes the unwanted areas, fixing keystone distortion in one step.

Resize pixel art in Photoshop without losing its crisp, blocky appearance by opening Image > Image Size, ensuring Resample is on, and entering a percentage that is a multiple of 100 (like 400%) into the Width and Height boxes. The default resampling method blends pixels, creating unwanted soft edges and halos, so you must change the Resample dropdown from Automatic to Nearest Neighbor (hard edges) to keep each pixel square and sharp. Use the preview window to check results before applying.

Open any image in Photoshop, go to Image > Image Size, and see that the Resolution value (e.g., 300 pixels/inch) only controls print size—dividing pixel dimensions by resolution gives inches on paper—and has zero effect on the file size in megabytes shown at the top of the dialog box. Lowering the resolution to 72 ppi does not shrink the file size because file size depends solely on pixel dimensions (width × height) and compression, not on how many pixels are assigned per inch. To prove it, change the Resolution field while keeping the pixel dimensions unchanged; the megabyte value stays identical, confirming that there is no such thing as a “web resolution” for faster downloads.

Open any image in Photoshop and go to Image > Image Size to see its current pixel dimensions and file size in megabytes. The file size depends on both the total pixel count and the RGB color model: each pixel requires 3 bytes of memory—one byte each for the Red, Green, and Blue color channels. To calculate the file size yourself, multiply the image’s width in pixels by its height to get the total number of pixels, then multiply that result by 3. The final number is the file size in bytes, which you can convert to megabytes by dividing by 1,048,576. This explains exactly where the Image Size dialog box gets its number.

Use the Image > Duplicate command to create a flattened copy of your photo, then open Image Size from the same menu. In the dialog box, ensure Dimensions are set to Pixels, then enter a new width—Photoshop automatically constrains proportions and updates the height. For email or sharing, a width around 600–800 pixels is typical; this simultaneously shrinks the file size in megabytes displayed at the top of the panel. Avoid resampling with the "Resample" checkbox unchecked, as that would only change the print resolution, not the pixel dimensions needed for screen viewing.

Open any image in Photoshop, go to Image > Image Size, and turn off the Resample checkbox to change only the print size without altering pixels. The Width, Height, and Resolution fields then show the current print dimensions; adjust the Resolution to 300 pixels per inch for standard frame sizes like 8x10 or 11x14. If the image is too small for the desired print, turn Resample back on and increase the pixel dimensions (upsampling) to enlarge it. For frame sizes that don’t match the photo’s aspect ratio, crop the image first using the Crop Tool before resizing.

Open the Image Size dialog box via Image > Image Size or Ctrl+Alt+I (Win) / Command+Option+I (Mac) to view and change an image’s dimensions and resolution. Use the redesigned preview window to see the image at an accurate 100% view; click and drag inside it to scroll, click directly on the image to jump the preview to that spot, or use the +/- buttons (or Ctrl/Command-click to zoom in, Alt/Option-click to zoom out) to adjust the zoom level. Resize the entire dialog box by dragging a corner for a larger preview. The right panel displays the current pixel dimensions and resolution, where you can choose preset sizes, enter custom Width/Height/Resolution values, and toggle the Resample option to control whether pixel data is added or removed during resizing.

Pixels are the tiny colored squares that form all digital images, and their grid becomes visible when zooming past 500% in Photoshop, though the Pixel Grid overlay can be toggled off via View > Show > Pixel Grid. Image size refers to the pixel width and height of an image, viewable in the Image Size dialog box (Image > Image Size), which also displays total pixel count. Image resolution controls print size by dictating how many pixels are squeezed into each inch, and contrary to popular belief, resolution has no effect on the file size of your image.

Cycle through all 27 layer blend modes from your keyboard by pressing Shift+Plus (next) or Shift+Minus (previous) while the Move Tool (V) or a selection tool is active—tools without their own blend modes prevent accidental tool-mode changes. Jump directly to a specific blend mode using shortcuts: Shift+Alt+N for Normal, Shift+Alt+M for Multiply, Shift+Alt+S for Screen, Shift+Alt+O for Overlay, Shift+Alt+F for Soft Light, and Shift+Alt+C for Color. Adjust a blend mode’s intensity by duplicating the layer, applying the blend mode to the copy, then lowering the copy’s Opacity slider. Memorize shortcuts faster by noting that the letter often matches the mode’s first letter (e.g., M for Multiply, S for Screen).

Press C to select the Crop Tool, then use Shift to lock the aspect ratio while dragging a corner handle, Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) to resize from the center, or both together. Tap X to swap between portrait and landscape orientation, H to hide or show the cropped area, and P to toggle Classic Mode so you move the crop border instead of the image. Hold Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) to temporarily access the Straighten Tool and drag along a horizon line to rotate the image, then release the key to return to cropping. Press Esc to cancel, and cycle through overlays like Rule of Thirds using the Overlay icon in the Options Bar.

Place your logo or copyright text as a linked smart object so that any edits to the original logo file instantly update across all images. First, create your logo in a separate Photoshop document with a transparent background (turn off the Background layer’s visibility icon) and save it as a .PSD. Open an image, go to File > Place Linked, select your logo file, and click Place. Use Free Transform to resize and reposition the watermark. Because it is a linked smart object (available only in Photoshop CC), editing the original .PSD file will automatically refresh the watermark in every document that links to it.

Select all layers you want to combine, then go to the Layers panel menu and choose Convert to Smart Object, or right-click and select Convert to Smart Object. This merges the layers into a single smart object that remains fully editable—double-click its thumbnail to open the embedded document, make changes to any original layer, save, and the main document updates instantly. Any filters applied to the smart object become editable smart filters, so you can sharpen or adjust non-destructively without deleting merged copies or redoing work. Use Shift+Ctrl+Alt+E (Win) / Shift+Command+Option+E (Mac) to merge layers onto a new layer, but converting to a smart object instead avoids the hassle of deleting and re-merging when edits are needed later.

Copy a smart object using either **Layer > New > Layer via Copy** (Ctrl+J) or **Layer > Smart Objects > New Smart Object via Copy**. New Layer via Copy creates a linked duplicate that shares the original’s content—editing one automatically updates the other. New Smart Object via Copy creates an independent duplicate; changes to either copy do not affect the other. Both commands are found under the Layer menu, and the Layers panel shows a smart object icon on each thumbnail.

Convert any layer into a smart object by right-clicking it in the Layers panel and choosing Convert to Smart Object, then use a layer mask to place the image inside a selection made with the Polygonal Lasso Tool. Double-click the smart object thumbnail to open its contents in a separate document window, where you can edit the image using any Photoshop tool or adjustment—changes save automatically back to the main document. To replace the contents entirely, right-click the smart object layer, choose Replace Contents, and select a new image file; the new image instantly appears inside the frame, preserving all masks, transforms, and filters applied to the smart object. This workflow turns smart objects into reusable templates, as the frame selection and layer mask remain intact while only the internal image changes.

Convert any layer into a smart object by renaming the Background layer and choosing Convert to Smart Object from the Layers panel menu, then apply filters from the Filter menu—such as Gaussian Blur—as editable, non-destructive smart filters. Smart filters appear beneath the smart object layer in the Layers panel, where you can double-click the filter name to reopen and adjust its settings at any time without quality loss, toggle visibility on or off, change blend mode and opacity, and reorder multiple filters by dragging them up or down. Each smart filter set includes a built-in layer mask, letting you paint with black on the mask to hide the filter effect from specific areas, and you can even apply Camera Raw Filter as a non-destructive smart filter for powerful, reversible edits.

Open an image directly as a smart object via File > Open as Smart Object, or convert any existing layer—including the Background layer—by using Layer > Smart Objects > Convert to Smart Object, right-clicking the layer in the Layers panel, or right-clicking the canvas with a selection tool active (except the Magic Wand). The smart object icon appears on the layer thumbnail, indicating the content is now protected inside a container that can be scaled, transformed, warped, distorted, or filtered without permanent damage or quality loss. Multiple copies of the same smart object update automatically when the source content is edited, making them ideal for reusable templates.

Blend two images in Photoshop using three methods. Start with the Opacity option in the Layers panel, lowering the value from 100% to make the top layer transparent and reveal the image below—press 1 for 10%, 2 for 20%, or two numbers quickly for precise values. Next, apply layer blend modes like Multiply or Screen from the blend mode dropdown to creatively merge images, especially textures with photos. Finally, use a layer mask by adding a white mask to the top layer and painting with a black brush to hide areas, revealing the bottom layer seamlessly. Each method offers distinct control for fading, texturing, or compositing images together.

Open the Brushes panel via Window > Brushes, click the panel menu icon in the upper right corner, and choose Get More Brushes to launch your browser to Adobe’s site where 15 brush sets from Kyle T. Webster are available for download. After downloading an .abr file, double-click it while Photoshop is running to install the set, which then appears as a new folder in the Brushes panel. This process gives Creative Cloud subscribers access to over 1000 additional brushes, including watercolor, spatter, impressionist, and manga styles, beyond the 20 default brushes included in the Dry Media, Wet Media, and Special Effects sets.

Save custom brushes as presets using the new Brushes panel in Photoshop CC 2018, which replaces the old Brush Presets panel and now saves Tool Settings from the Options Bar (Mode, Opacity, Flow, Smoothing) plus brush color alongside standard Brush Settings. After setting up a brush—for example, a highlighter with Multiply mode, 60% Opacity, 100 px size, 77° Angle, 20% Roundness, 100% Hardness, and 10% Spacing—open the Brushes panel via Window > Brushes, click the Create New Group icon to make a folder for organization, select that group, then click the Create New Brush icon to save the preset with all settings and color included.

Open the Brushes panel menu or the Brush Preset Picker gear icon, choose Legacy Brushes from the list, and click OK to restore all of Photoshop's classic brush sets—including Assorted Brushes, Natural Brushes, and Special Effect Brushes—that were removed starting in CC 2018. The loaded sets appear inside a new Legacy Brushes folder below the default folders in both the Brushes panel and the Brush Preset Picker, where you can twirl open any set to select individual brushes.

Select the image you need to resize by clicking its document tab, then open Image > Image Size. Instead of typing dimensions manually, go to the Window menu and choose the name of the other open image from the list at the bottom—Photoshop instantly updates the Width and Height fields in the Image Size dialog to match that image’s pixel dimensions. Click OK to apply the resize. This trick works with any number of open documents and avoids writing down or retyping values.

Copy the Background layer (Select > All, then Edit > Copy) from the open document, then choose File > New; Photoshop automatically fills the Width, Height, and Resolution fields in the New Document dialog box to match the copied layer’s dimensions, so you can click Create without manually entering or memorizing any numbers.

Sample a single color for all type or different colors for individual letters by clicking the Type Tool’s color swatch in the Options Bar to open the Color Picker, then moving the cursor over the image—it becomes the Eyedropper Tool—and clicking to sample any pixel. For multi-color text, highlight a single letter with the Type Tool, click the color swatch again, sample a new area, and click the checkmark in the Options Bar to accept. The Color Picker remains open until you click OK, so you can click multiple spots in the image to preview the color before committing.

Hold the Shift key while choosing Select and Mask from the Select menu to open the classic Refine Edge dialog box instead of the Select and Mask workspace. This trick works in Photoshop CC 2018 and later versions, but requires an initial selection made beforehand with a tool like Color Range or the Quick Selection Tool, as Refine Edge cannot create selections from scratch.

Drag and drop, copy-paste, or duplicate a layer to move an image between Photoshop documents. Use **Select > All** and **Edit > Copy/Paste** to transfer a full image onto its own new layer. Duplicate a layer by right-clicking it in the Layers panel and choosing **Duplicate Layer**, then selecting the destination document from the dialog. For drag and drop, arrange documents as tabbed windows, in a multi-document layout (via **Window > Arrange**), or as floating windows; then drag the layer from the Layers panel or the image itself onto the target document’s canvas. A key caveat: dragging between tabbed documents requires hovering over the target tab until the document switches, then releasing.

Enlarge images in Photoshop without losing quality using Preserve Details 2.0, a technology preview available in Photoshop CC 2018 and later. First, enable it under Edit (Windows) or Photoshop CC (Mac) > Preferences > Technology Previews. Open Image Size from the Image menu, ensure Resample is checked, and enter your new Width and Height (e.g., 400%). Change the resample method from Automatic to Preserve Details 2.0, then adjust the Reduce Noise slider to balance sharpness and smoothness. This algorithm intelligently adds pixels to preserve fine detail and texture, avoiding the soft or chunky results of older upsampling methods.

If the Curvature Pen Tool is missing from your Toolbar after upgrading to Photoshop CC 2018, it is likely because you are using a custom workspace saved from an earlier version. Restore it by clicking the Edit Toolbar icon (three dots) near the bottom of the Toolbar, or go to Edit > Toolbar. In the Customize Toolbar dialog box, drag the Curvature Pen Tool from the Extra Tools column into the Toolbar column, placing it between the Freeform Pen Tool and the Add Anchor Point Tool. Click Done, and the tool will now appear nested behind the standard Pen Tool.

Use the Curvature Pen Tool in Photoshop CC 2018 to draw paths by clicking to add anchor points; Photoshop automatically generates smooth curves between them. Set the Tool Mode to "Path" in the Options Bar before starting, then click to place points—the tool draws curved lines by default. To switch between curved and straight segments, double-click any anchor point to toggle its corner point property. After completing a path, convert it into a selection, vector mask, or shape via the right-click context menu or the Properties panel. The tool is nested behind the standard Pen Tool in the Toolbar, and you can add guide layouts (View > New Guide Layout) to help align points precisely.

Hover over any tool in the Toolbar to see the new Rich Tool Tips in Photoshop CC 2018—large pop-ups that include a short video animation of the tool in action, along with its name, keyboard shortcut, and description. To disable this feature and restore the traditional, compact text-only Tool Tips, open Preferences (Edit > Preferences > Tools on Windows; Photoshop CC > Preferences > Tools on Mac) and uncheck "Use Rich Tooltips".

Navigate images faster using keyboard shortcuts and tool tricks. Press Ctrl+0 (Win) / Command+0 (Mac) to fit the image on screen, or Ctrl+1 / Command+1 for 100% view. Temporarily switch to the Zoom Tool from any other tool by holding Ctrl+spacebar (Win) / Command+spacebar (Mac) to zoom in, or add Alt/Option to zoom out. Use the Hand Tool by holding the spacebar to pan, or double-click it for Fit on Screen. The Navigator panel offers a thumbnail for quick zoom and pan control, while the Rotate View Tool lets you non-destructively rotate the canvas using the R key.

Use the Rotate View Tool (nested behind the Hand Tool in the Toolbar, shortcut R) to rotate the canvas without altering the image pixels, preserving quality. Click and drag inside the document window to freely rotate the view; a compass appears with a red marker always pointing to the image’s true top. Hold Shift while dragging to snap rotation in 15-degree increments. For precise angles, enter a value in the Rotation Angle box in the Options Bar, or use the Scrubby Slider by clicking and dragging left or right over the “Rotation Angle” label, with Shift held for finer control.

Enable Overscroll in Photoshop’s Preferences (Edit/Photoshop menu > Preferences > Tools) to pan or scroll an image with the Hand Tool even when the entire image fits on screen. With Overscroll off, Photoshop prevents any movement at Fit on Screen (View > Fit on Screen or Ctrl+0/Cmd+0). Turning it on lets you drag any edge or corner area—like a subject positioned off-center—into the middle of the document window for retouching without zooming in.

Open the Navigator panel from the Window menu, then resize it by dragging its bottom-left corner for a larger preview. Zoom by dragging the slider bar, clicking the mountain icons, entering a specific percentage in the lower-left field, or using the hidden scrubby slider (Ctrl/Command-click and drag on the zoom value). The red view box in the preview window shows which portion of the image is visible in the document window; click and drag that box to pan around when zoomed in.

Switch between Photoshop’s three Screen Modes—Standard Screen Mode, Full Screen Mode With Menu Bar, and Full Screen Mode—to hide interface elements and reclaim screen space for your image. Access them from the View menu or the Screen Mode icon at the bottom of the Toolbar. Standard Screen Mode shows the entire interface, including the Toolbar, panels, Menu Bar, Options Bar, tab, scroll bars, and Status Bar. Full Screen Mode With Menu Bar hides the Toolbar and panels but keeps the Menu Bar; Full Screen Mode hides everything except the image. Use the F key to cycle through modes, and press Tab to toggle all panels on or off. Note that Screen Modes control interface visibility, while View Modes (like Fit on Screen or 100%) control zoom level independently.

Switch between Photoshop’s built-in workspaces—Essentials, 3D, Graphic and Web, Motion, Painting, and Photography—by going to Window > Workspace or clicking the Workspace icon in the upper right of the interface. Each workspace rearranges the panels, Toolbar, menu items, and keyboard shortcuts to suit specific tasks, like using the Layers, Adjustments, and Color panels in Essentials for general editing or different panels for painting or photography. Save your own custom workspace after arranging panels and tools exactly how you want them, then update or delete it from the same menu. Restore the default Essentials workspace anytime to reset the panel layout.

Use the Window > Arrange menu to select a multi-document layout like 2-up Vertical or 2-up Horizontal, which displays all open images simultaneously in resizable panels. Only the document whose tab is highlighted is editable; click a tab or inside a window to make it active. Layouts for three or more documents are grayed out until enough images are open.

Drag a document tab down and away from the tab bar to turn it into a floating window, or drag a floating window's title bar back into the tab bar to re-dock it. Switch between tabbed documents by clicking their tabs or using Ctrl+Tab (Win) / Control+Tab (Mac); add Shift to reverse direction. Reorder tabs by dragging them horizontally—dragging diagonally accidentally creates a floating window. To have new images open as floating windows by default, disable "Open Documents as Tabs" in Photoshop's Preferences under the Interface panel.

Open the panel area along the right of the interface, then show, hide, and rearrange panels by dragging their tabs into or out of panel groups. Use the workspace selection icon in the upper right to choose the Essentials workspace, then reset it via the menu to restore the default layout of the Color, Properties, and Layers panels in the main column. Panels like Swatches and Libraries appear as additional tabs within groups; drag a tab to move it between columns or create its own floating group.

Right-click the toolbar’s three-dot menu and choose Reset Toolbar to restore every tool slot to its default primary tool, or use Edit > Toolbar to drag tools back manually. For tool-specific options, right-click any tool icon in the Options Bar and pick Reset Tool to clear custom settings for that single tool, or choose Reset All Tools from the same menu to reset every tool’s Options Bar defaults at once—a feature that, since Photoshop CC 2014, also resets the toolbar layout, not just the settings.

Navigate the Photoshop interface by identifying its core components: the Document window with its tab, zoom level, and Status Bar (which reveals image dimensions and color profile when clicked); the Toolbar, expandable via double-arrows at the top; the Options Bar, which updates options for the currently selected tool; the Menu Bar for file, edit, and layer commands; and the right-side panel area. Use the Search feature to quickly find tools or commands, and switch between pre-built Workspaces to customize the layout. This overview applies to both Photoshop CC and CS6.

Press and hold Shift+Ctrl+Alt (Win) / Shift+Command+Option (Mac) while relaunching Photoshop to trigger a prompt asking to delete the Adobe Photoshop Settings file; choosing Yes resets all preferences, color settings, custom keyboard shortcuts, and workspaces to defaults. In Photoshop CC, open Edit > Preferences > General (Win) / Photoshop CC > Preferences > General (Mac), enable the new Reset Preferences On Quit option, confirm, then quit and relaunch Photoshop to achieve the same reset.

Open the Photoshop Preferences (Edit/Photoshop > Preferences > General) and disable Export Clipboard to prevent performance issues from huge files being sent to your system clipboard. Switch to the Interface category and click one of the four Color Theme swatches to change the overall shade of gray in the workspace, with the default being the second darkest swatch from the left in both Photoshop CC and CS6.

Synchronize Photoshop’s color settings with other Creative Cloud apps like Illustrator and InDesign by using Adobe Bridge, not Photoshop itself. First, confirm your custom color preset (e.g., “My Color Settings” with Adobe RGB as the RGB working space and Preserve Embedded Profiles active) is selected in Photoshop’s Edit > Color Settings dialog. Then open Adobe Bridge via File > Browse in Bridge, go to Edit > Creative Suite Color Settings, choose your preset, and click Apply—the Suite Color Settings dialog will show all apps now synced. A key caveat: the sync option is only available in Bridge, not within Photoshop, and you must have Bridge installed separately for Creative Cloud subscribers.

Open Photoshop's Color Settings from the Edit menu and change the RGB working space from the default sRGB to Adobe RGB (1998) to access a wider color gamut with richer, more saturated colors and greater detail. The Working Spaces section under the Color Settings dialog box controls which color space Photoshop uses for different tasks; only the RGB setting matters for on-screen editing, while CMYK, Gray, and Spot can stay at their defaults. This single switch expands the range of colors available without altering any other advanced options, and the change applies to all images you open thereafter.

Use Adobe Bridge as a digital asset manager to find, organize, and preview images without opening Photoshop. Navigate the Bridge interface and panels to browse files, then customize thumbnail sizes with a slider and view larger previews in the Preview panel. Jump to a Full Screen Preview for a closer look or enter Review Mode to cycle through a series, keeping only the keepers. Apply one-to-five-star ratings or color labels (e.g., yellow for "needs work," green for "approved") to sort images, and add keywords or copyright information via the Metadata panel. Filter images by rating, label, or other criteria to quickly locate specific files.

Use Adobe Bridge’s built-in Photo Downloader to transfer images from a camera or memory card to your computer. Launch Bridge from Photoshop via **File > Browse in Bridge**, then open the Photo Downloader by choosing **File > Get Photos from Camera** or clicking the camera icon in Bridge’s toolbar. Select your camera or card from the **Get Photos from** dropdown; if it does not appear, click **Refresh List**. Mac users can set the Photo Downloader to launch automatically when a camera is connected by toggling **When a Camera is Connected, Launch Adobe Photo Downloader** in Bridge’s Preferences under the **Adobe Bridge CC** menu.

Install Adobe Bridge, a powerful file browser included with your Creative Cloud subscription, by opening the Creative Cloud desktop app from Photoshop's Help > Updates menu. Scroll through the available apps list until you find Bridge, then click the Install button. Once the installation completes, Bridge moves to your installed apps list where you can open it directly from the Creative Cloud app or later from within Photoshop.

Close a single image using File > Close, Ctrl+W (Win) / Command+W (Mac), or the “x” on the document tab. Close all open images at once with File > Close All or Ctrl+Alt+W / Command+Option+W. An asterisk in the tab signals unsaved changes; choosing No/Don’t Save still closes the image and discards edits permanently, so use Cancel to abort. When closing multiple unsaved images, check Apply to All to apply the same save/don’t-save choice to every file. If you opened images from Adobe Bridge, use File > Close and Go to Bridge to return directly to Bridge.

Double-click a raw file’s thumbnail in Adobe Bridge’s Content panel to open it directly in Camera Raw; JPEGs and TIFFs require a different method. For JPEGs, right-click the thumbnail in Bridge, choose **Open in Camera Raw** from the context menu, or use the keyboard shortcut **Ctrl+R** (Win) / **Cmd+R** (Mac). The same right-click approach works for TIFFs. Once inside Camera Raw, click **Open Image** to send the file to Photoshop for further editing, or click **Done** to save your non-destructive adjustments and return to Bridge without opening Photoshop.

Open Adobe Bridge's Preferences panel, select the File Type Associations section, and reassign any misbehaving file extension—such as PNG, TIFF, or JPEG—to the correct version of Photoshop by clicking its current application entry and choosing Photoshop from the pop-up menu. This fixes the problem when Bridge opens images in the wrong program or in an older Photoshop version instead of the newest one. The change applies immediately to all future files of that type, and you can verify the fix by double-clicking a thumbnail in Bridge's Content panel to see the image open in the intended Photoshop version.

Open Adobe Bridge from Photoshop via File > Browse in Bridge, then use the Folders panel (click its tab next to Favorites) to navigate your computer’s directory tree. Click triangles to expand folders and locate your images. Once you find the folder, select an image in the Content panel and press Ctrl+Enter (Windows) or Cmd+Return (Mac) to open it directly into Photoshop, or right-click and choose Open. Bridge CC must be installed separately through the Creative Cloud app (Help > Updates in Photoshop), even though it is included free with all Creative Cloud subscriptions.

Use the Crop Tool to expand canvas space around an image instead of just trimming it. First, unlock the Background layer by clicking its lock icon (CC) or Alt/Option-double-clicking the name (CS6 and earlier). Select the Crop Tool (C), then drag any crop handle outward to add blank, transparent space. Hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) while dragging a handle to resize the crop border symmetrically from the center, or hold Shift to constrain proportions. Press Enter to commit the crop, then add a new solid-color fill layer beneath the image layer to turn the extra space into a colored border.

Rotate an image by hand using the Crop Tool, then straighten it more accurately with the Straighten Tool, or use the Ruler Tool for the best precision. With the Crop Tool selected, move the cursor outside the crop border until the Rotate icon appears, then click and drag to rotate, aligning a horizontal or vertical element with the grid lines. For a faster, more accurate straighten, click the Straighten icon in the Options Bar and drag along something that should be straight. The Ruler Tool, hidden behind the Eyedropper Tool, lets you click and drag along a crooked edge, then click Straighten Layer in the Options Bar to automatically rotate and crop the image.

Open Photoshop's Preferences (Edit > Preferences > General on Windows, Photoshop CC > Preferences > General on Mac) and check the "Use Legacy 'New Document' Interface" box to instantly switch from the redesigned New Document dialog box to the smaller, original version. The legacy dialog box opens with your last-used settings or Photoshop's defaults, and you can name your document in the Name field or skip it and name it later when saving. Select a document type from the Document Type dropdown to choose from built-in presets like Photo, Web, Mobile, and Art & Illustration, or create your own custom presets.

Enable the hidden Recent Files workspace in Photoshop CC via Edit (Win) / Photoshop CC (Mac) > Preferences > General, checking “Show 'Recent Files' Workspace When Opening A File,” then quit and relaunch. With the workspace active, choose File > Open (or press Ctrl+O / Cmd+O) to display recent files as clickable thumbnails in a panel, even while other documents remain open—unlike the Start screen, which only appears when no documents are open. The panel avoids the name-only list of File > Open Recent, letting you visually identify and reopen images without closing your current document.

Disable the Start workspace in Photoshop CC by going to Edit (Win) or Photoshop CC (Mac) > Preferences > General and unchecking “Show ‘START’ Workspace When No Documents Are Open,” then quit and relaunch Photoshop to see an empty workspace instead. Without the Start screen, create new documents via File > New or Ctrl+N (Win) / Command+N (Mac), open existing images with File > Open or Ctrl+O (Win) / Command+O (Mac), and reopen recent files from File > Open Recent.

Open the Start workspace in Photoshop CC 2017 to create new documents or open existing images. Click the **New...** button on the left or the **Start new** button in the center to launch the completely redesigned **New Document** dialog box. Use the **Open...** button to browse for files, or select a recently-opened file from the **RECENT FILES** list. The new **CC FILES** option lets you open PSDs stored in your Creative Cloud cloud storage. Dynamic tiles along the bottom offer Adobe Stock image search, tutorials, and downloadable content; click **Show All** to view more tiles and **Back** to return.

Right-click an image file in Windows File Explorer, choose Properties, click the Change button next to “Opens with,” and select Photoshop from the list—if Photoshop isn’t listed, click More Apps to find it. On a Mac, Control-click the file, select Get Info, expand the “Open with” section, choose Photoshop from the dropdown, and click Change All to apply the setting to all files of that type. Repeat these steps separately for each file extension you want to open in Photoshop, such as JPEG, PNG, and TIFF.

Right-click an image file in Windows File Explorer, choose Properties, click the Change button next to “Opens with,” and select Photoshop from the list; if Photoshop isn’t listed, click More Apps to find it. On a Mac, Control-click the image file, select Get Info, expand the “Open with” section, choose Photoshop from the dropdown menu, then click Change All to apply the setting to all files of that type. Repeat these steps separately for each file extension—such as JPEG, PNG, and TIFF—to make Photoshop the default editor for all your image formats.

Press F7 to toggle the Layers panel, hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) while clicking the New Layer icon to name a layer before creation, or use Shift+Ctrl+N (Win) / Shift+Command+N (Mac) to open the New Layer dialog from the keyboard. Bypass naming with Shift+Ctrl+Alt+N (Win) / Shift+Command+Option+N (Mac). Copy a layer or selection to a new layer with Ctrl+J (Win) / Command+J (Mac); add Ctrl+Alt+J (Win) / Command+Option+J to name the copy first. Copy and move a layer simultaneously by selecting the Move Tool (V), then Alt-dragging (Win) / Option-dragging (Mac) the layer in the document. To add a new layer below the currently selected one, hold Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) while clicking the New Layer icon.

Convert the existing Background layer into a normal layer by double-clicking its name in the Layers panel and clicking OK, then create a new Background layer from scratch by choosing Layer > New > Background From Layer. To save these steps as a reusable action, open the Actions panel (Window > Actions), click the New Action icon, name it, click Record, perform the two steps, then click Stop. Before recording, press D to reset the Foreground and Background colors to their defaults (white for the Background color) or press X to swap them to black. The new Background layer will fill with whichever Background color is active.

Open an image in Photoshop and it lands on a locked Background layer that cannot be moved, have transparent pixels, or be reordered. Use the Move Tool to drag the layer and Photoshop pops a warning listing these restrictions, with a Convert to Normal Layer button new in CC. Instead of clicking that button, unlock the layer by double-clicking its name in the Layers panel and clicking OK in the New Layer dialog, instantly converting it to a normal layer that supports transparency, repositioning, and stacking freely.

Navigate the Layers panel by opening it from the Window menu or locating it in the lower-right panel group, where each layer appears as a row with a name, a preview thumbnail, and controls for visibility, blend modes, opacity, locking, and fill.
