Add new layers using the New Layer button, delete them by dragging to the trash icon, and reorder layers by dragging rows up or down. Group layers by selecting multiple and pressing Ctrl+G (Cmd+G on Mac), and apply layer masks or adjustment layers from the icons at the bottom of the panel. The Background layer is a special locked type that cannot be moved or have transparency until you convert it to a normal layer by double-clicking its name.

Draw a square selection with the Rectangular Marquee Tool, fill it via Edit > Fill using the Color Picker, then add a circle with the Elliptical Marquee Tool and a different color. Without layers, moving or editing one shape affects the other because both exist on the same Background layer. Recreate the composition with layers by placing each shape on its own new layer, using the Layers panel to select, move, and reorder them independently. The Layers panel, accessed via Window > Layers, lets you add layers, rename them, change opacity, and toggle visibility, making non-destructive editing possible.

Select the Ellipse Tool, set its Tool Mode to Path in the Options Bar, then Shift+Alt/Option-drag from the center of your image to draw a perfect circular path. Switch to the Type Tool, choose your font and size, and move the cursor directly over the path until it becomes an I-beam with a dotted wavy line. Click on the path and begin typing; the text will follow the path’s shape. You can reposition the text later by dragging its start or end marker with the Path Selection Tool.

Convert the Type layer to a Smart Object to apply any Photoshop filter—like Gaussian Blur—as a fully editable Smart Filter, keeping the text live and editable. The Smart Object icon appears on the layer thumbnail, and the Smart Filter appears below the layer in the Layers panel with a built-in layer mask, adjustable blend mode, and independent opacity. Unlike rasterizing, this method lets you re-open the filter dialog at any time, toggle effects on/off, and stack multiple filters without permanent changes.

Right-click a JPEG in Lightroom’s Develop module, go to the Photo menu, choose Edit In > Edit in Adobe Photoshop CC, and Lightroom sends a pixel-based copy to Photoshop with all Develop module adjustments baked in. Unlike raw files, JPEGs do not open as Camera Raw smart objects; the copy is a flattened TIFF or PSD (set in Lightroom’s External Editing preferences). After editing in Photoshop—for example, using the Clone Stamp or Spot Healing Brush to remove wires—simply save the file (File > Save). The edited version automatically appears back in Lightroom’s catalog next to the original, with “-Edit” appended to the filename. This round-trip workflow works identically for TIFFs and PSDs, letting you leverage Lightroom’s non-destructive global adjustments and Photoshop’s pixel-level retouching without ever altering the original JPEG.

Right-click the raw file in Lightroom’s Develop module or Library grid and choose **Edit In** > **Edit in Adobe Photoshop CC**. Lightroom automatically creates a copy of the raw file, converts it to a TIFF or PSD (set in **Preferences** > **External Editing**), and opens that copy in Photoshop. After making pixel-level edits using layers, masks, selections, or filters, simply press **Ctrl+S** (Win) / **Cmd+S** (Mac) to save. The saved TIFF or PSD is instantly added back into the Lightroom catalog, stacked next to the original raw file, preserving all metadata and organization.

Open the Customize Toolbar dialog box via Edit > Toolbar or by right-clicking the ellipsis icon below the Zoom Tool and choosing Edit Toolbar. Drag any tool from the Extra Tools list on the right into the Toolbar list on the left to add it, or drag a tool out of the Toolbar list to hide it. Reorder tools by dragging them up or down in the left column, and group or ungroup them by dragging one tool onto another or away from its group. Save your custom layout as a preset using the Presets menu at the top of the dialog box, then load it later or restore defaults from the same menu. Keyboard shortcuts for individual tools remain unchanged unless you reassign them separately in the Keyboard Shortcuts dialog.

Open Photoshop CC 2015's Preferences (Edit > Preferences > Interface on Windows, Photoshop > Preferences > Interface on Mac) and choose one of four color themes to adjust the brightness of the entire interface, including dialog boxes that previously remained stubbornly light. The November 2015 update finally darkened all dialog boxes—like Smart Sharpen—to match the main interface, making it easier to focus on the image preview. If you prefer the old look, simply select a lighter color theme to brighten everything back up.

Open Photoshop CC 2015 and use the new Start screen and Recent Files panel to quickly access recently-opened images or create new documents without the traditional Tools or Layers panels cluttering the view. The Start screen appears on launch or when closing the last open document, displaying a list of recent files in either List View or Thumbnail View via the two icons above the column. Switch between the Start workspace and the familiar Essentials workspace using the Workspace menu in the upper right to toggle the Start screen on or off. Note that thumbnails only appear after you have opened a file in this version.

Use Free Transform (Ctrl+T / Command+T) to move, scale, rotate, skew, distort, add perspective, or warp any layer, selection, shape, type, layer mask, or vector mask. Access it from the Edit menu or with the keyboard shortcut. A bounding box with handles appears around your object; drag a corner handle to scale proportionally (hold Shift), drag a side handle to stretch, or move the pivot point to change the center of rotation. Right-click inside the bounding box to switch between transformation modes like Skew, Distort, Perspective, and Warp without leaving Free Transform. Apply multiple transformations as a single step to minimize quality loss, or convert your layer to a Smart Object first for fully non-destructive editing. Note that in Photoshop CC 2019, the default behavior changed to scale proportionally without holding Shift, so check your version’s preferences if the handles behave unexpectedly.

Blend two or more photos into a seamless composite by combining gradients with layer masks in Photoshop. Place each image on its own layer, then add a layer mask to the top image you want to blend. Use the Gradient Tool set to the Foreground to Background gradient (black to white) and drag across the mask to hide or reveal portions of the layer—black conceals, white reveals. The key distinction is that Foreground to Background creates a smooth, opaque-to-opaque transition, while Foreground to Transparent fades the image into transparency, which is useful when you need the underlying layers to show through without a hard edge. Control the blend by adjusting the gradient’s start and end points, and toggle layer visibility to preview each image individually.

Open the Gradient Editor by clicking the gradient preview bar in the Options Bar with the Gradient Tool active, then select any preset gradient as a starting point. Edit colors by double-clicking the color stops below the gradient bar to open the Color Picker, and add or remove stops by clicking below the bar or dragging a stop away. Adjust the transition between colors by dragging the diamond-shaped midpoint markers, and control the smoothness of the blend using the Smoothness value in the Gradient Editor. Add transparency by adjusting the opacity stops above the bar, and save your custom gradient as a new preset by typing a name and clicking the New button.

Select the Custom Shape Tool from the Tools panel by clicking and holding the Rectangle Tool icon, then set the Tool Mode to Shape in the Options Bar to draw resolution-independent vector shapes. Click the shape thumbnail in the Options Bar to open the Custom Shape Picker, then click the gear icon and choose All from the list to load every shape set at once, clicking Append to add them without replacing the defaults.

Vector shapes, made from mathematical points, remain crisp at any size, while pixel shapes become blurry when scaled. Use the Free Transform Path command (Edit > Free Transform Path or Ctrl+T/Command+T) to scale a vector shape layer, identified by the shape icon in the layer preview thumbnail. Scaling a pixel shape with Free Transform (Edit > Free Transform) reveals jagged edges and lost detail, demonstrating why vector shapes are preferable for logos, UI designs, and any artwork requiring resizing.

Draw basic geometric vector shapes in Photoshop CS6 using the Rectangle, Rounded Rectangle, Ellipse, Polygon, and Line tools, selecting **Shape** from the Tool Mode option in the Options Bar to ensure resolution-independent, scalable results. Set a fill color by clicking the Fill swatch and choosing No Color, Solid Color, Gradient, or Pattern, then add a stroke with customizable appearance. Shape layers remain editable later, allowing you to adjust fill, stroke, and path properties without losing quality.

Draw vector shapes and pixel shapes in Photoshop CS6, then compare how they behave when scaled. Use the Shape tool to create a vector shape on a Shape layer, identified by the small shape icon in the layer preview thumbnail, and a pixel shape on a normal layer. Scale the vector shape to 10% of its original size using Edit > Free Transform Path (or Ctrl+T/Command+T) and entering Width and Height values in the Options Bar, then scale it back up to 100%—the vector shape remains crisp and sharp because it is based on mathematical points, not pixels. In contrast, scaling a pixel shape down and then back up reveals permanent quality loss, as pixels cannot be recreated once discarded.

Open the New Guide Layout dialog box from the View menu, then set any number of columns and rows with exact widths, heights, gutters, and margins—all from one panel. Check Clear Existing Guides to remove old guides before applying the new layout, and use the Preview option to see changes live on your document. Save custom layouts as presets for reuse. This feature is available only in Photoshop CC (2014 or later) and requires an active Creative Cloud subscription.

Right-click any image file in Windows Explorer, choose Open with > Choose default program, select Adobe Photoshop from the list (click More options if needed), and check the box to always use this app. First enable file name extensions via the View menu so you can identify .jpg, .png, or .tif files. This overrides Windows Photo Viewer or the Photos app, making Photoshop the default viewer and editor for that specific format.

Resize the Color panel in Photoshop CC 2014 by dragging its left or bottom edge, or undock it by dragging the name tab to resize independently. The panel now includes two new color-selection modes borrowed from the Color Picker: the Hue Cube and Brightness Cube. Switch to these via the panel menu to choose colors by clicking directly on a hue strip and a brightness/saturation square, making the larger panel size genuinely useful for more intuitive color picking without opening the Color Picker.

Use Focus Area to create an initial depth-of-field selection, then click the Refine Edge button in the dialog box to transfer it. Inside Refine Edge, cycle through View Modes (On White, On Black, On Layers) by pressing F, and temporarily access the Zoom Tool with Ctrl+spacebar (Win) / Command+spacebar (Mac) or the Hand Tool with the spacebar alone. The critical Edge Detection section analyzes tone and color transitions around the selection outline to find true edges, while the Adjust Edge and Output sections handle fine-tuning and final output. Refine Edge cannot make initial selections—it only refines edges from an existing selection.

Use Select > Focus Area to let Photoshop CC 2014 automatically analyze an image and create a selection based on the in-focus region, ideal for isolating a sharp subject against a blurred background. After the initial selection appears in the Focus Area dialog box, adjust the In-Focus Range slider to expand or contract which areas are considered sharp enough to include. Change the selection’s preview background via the View thumbnail (press F to cycle through On White, On Black, Overlay, On Layers, or Black & White). The initial selection often requires further refinement, which is handled in a second step using the Refine Edge command.

Open the Layers Panel Options dialog box by clicking the menu icon in the top-right corner of the Layers panel and choosing Panel Options, or right-click/Control-click empty space below the bottom layer. Choose Small, Medium, Large, or No Thumbnails to control preview thumbnail size. To prevent Photoshop from automatically adding a layer mask to every new Fill or Adjustment layer, uncheck the "Add 'mask' by default to adjustment layers" and "Add 'mask' by default to fill layers" options in the same dialog box, keeping the panel uncluttered when masks are unnecessary.

Zoom in and out using the document tab's zoom level box in the lower-left corner—double-click it, type a value, and press Enter. For faster control, hover over that box, hold Ctrl (Win) or Command (Mac) to activate the scrubby slider, then drag right to zoom in or left to zoom out; add Shift to jump in 10% increments. Master the essential keyboard shortcuts: Ctrl++ (Win) / Command++ (Mac) to zoom in and Ctrl+- (Win) / Command+- (Mac) to zoom out, noting that these commands snap to preset percentages like 25%, 33.3%, or 50%. The View menu also offers Zoom In and Zoom Out commands, but the shortcuts and scrubby slider are far more efficient for daily navigation.

Zoom in and out using the Zoom Tool from the toolbar, or press Ctrl++ (Win) / Command++ (Mac) to zoom in and Ctrl+- (Win) / Command+- (Mac) to zoom out, with the zoom level displayed in the document tab and bottom-left corner. Double-click the bottom-left zoom value to type a custom percentage, or hover over it while holding Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) to activate the scrubby slider—drag right to zoom in, left to zoom out, and add Shift for 10% increments. Use the Hand Tool (H) to pan, or hold the Spacebar to temporarily switch from any tool to the Hand Tool for scrolling.

Save any custom panel arrangement as a named workspace via the workspace selection box in the top right corner of the interface, then instantly recall it later from the same drop-down menu. Photoshop CS6 includes built-in workspaces like Essentials and Painting, each displaying a task-specific set of panel groups in the main and secondary columns. To create your own, arrange panels (moving them between groups, collapsing them into icon view, or closing them) and choose **New Workspace** from the menu; the saved layout preserves panel positions, keyboard shortcuts, and customized menus. Revert to the default layout at any time by selecting **Reset Essentials** from the workspace menu.

Reset the Essentials workspace from the workspace selection box in the top-right corner, then choose Reset Essentials to restore default panel positions. Panels appear in two columns on the right, with the main column showing the Color, Adjustments, and Layers panels by default. Multiple panels are grouped into panel groups, indicated by tabs at the top; click any tab to bring that panel to the front of its group, while dimmed tabs show hidden panels.

Right-click a JPEG (or PNG, TIFF) thumbnail in Windows File Explorer, choose Properties, click the Change button next to “Opens with,” and select Photoshop from the list—if it’s missing, click More Apps to find it. On a Mac, Control-click the file, select Get Info, expand the “Open with” section, choose Photoshop from the dropdown, then click Change All to apply the setting to all files of that type. Repeat these steps for each image format you want Photoshop to handle by default.

Place a photo inside text by creating a clipping mask between a Type layer and an image layer. After adding your text with the Type Tool, resize it using Free Transform (Edit > Free Transform or Ctrl+T/Command+T). Position the image layer directly above the Type layer in the Layers panel, then right-click the image layer and choose "Create Clipping Mask" (or press Ctrl+Alt+G / Command+Option+G). The image will only appear within the letter shapes, while areas outside the text remain hidden. Choose a thick font like Impact so more of the image is visible through the letters.

Save large Photoshop files without interruption using CS6’s Background Save, which lets you continue editing—even adding adjustment layers—while the file writes in the background, indicated by a percentage and progress bar in the document window’s name tab and bottom-left corner. Enable Auto Save via Edit > Preferences > File Handling to set an interval (e.g., every 5, 10, 15, or 30 minutes); if Photoshop crashes, the next launch prompts you to recover the auto-saved version, preventing total loss of unsaved work. Both features are on by default, but you can disable Background Save in the same Preferences panel if needed.

Switch between four interface color themes in Photoshop CS6 by going to Edit > Preferences > Interface (PC) or Photoshop > Preferences > Interface (Mac) and clicking one of the four gray thumbnails, or cycle through them directly with Shift+F2 (darker to lighter) and Shift+F1 (lighter to darker). Change the pasteboard color independently by right-clicking (Win) or Control-clicking (Mac) in the canvas area and choosing Black, Dark Gray, Medium Gray, Light Gray, or Select Custom Color to open the Color Picker, though sticking with neutral grays avoids color interference with your image.

Use the Color Range command (Select > Color Range) to make tone- and color-based selections that are far more flexible and accurate than the Magic Wand. Set the Select option to Sampled Colors, then click on the image with the Eyedropper Tool to sample a color; hold Shift to add more colors with the Add to Sample Tool, or Alt to remove colors with the Subtract from Sample Tool. Unlike the Magic Wand, Color Range is a command accessed from the Menu Bar, not a tool in the Tools panel, and it offers additional preset options like Reds, Yellows, Highlights, and Shadows for instant selections.

Use the Type Tool with a Type layer selected, then click the Warp Text icon (a T with a curved line) in the Options Bar to open the Warp Text dialog box. Choose from 15 warp styles like Arc, then adjust the intensity with the Bend slider, dragging left for negative values to reverse the curve direction. The text remains 100% live and editable, and the warp effect itself stays fully adjustable—you can change styles, tweak settings, or turn warping off entirely without any loss of quality.

Select the Type Tool, choose a font and color, then sample a color directly from the image using the Eyedropper Tool while the Color Picker is open. Instead of guessing a font size in the Options Bar, select the Type layer in the Layers panel and press Ctrl+T (Win) / Command+T (Mac) to activate Free Transform. Drag any corner handle to scale the text to any size with a live preview, avoiding the trial-and-error of preset sizes. Because type is vector-based, scaling it this way remains crisp at any size.

Feather selection edges with a live preview by switching to Quick Mask mode (press Q) instead of using Select > Modify > Feather, which forces blind guessing. After making an elliptical selection, enter Quick Mask to see the selection as a red overlay; the softness of the transition appears as a gradient between red and clear areas. Double-click the Quick Mask icon to adjust the color and opacity of the overlay for better visibility. Paint with a soft-edged brush in Quick Mask to manually refine the feathered edge, or use Gaussian Blur (Filter > Blur > Gaussian Blur) on the Quick Mask to apply precise, previewable feathering. Press Q again to return to the standard selection, now with perfectly softened edges ready for your vignette or composite.

Hold down the Shift key to add to an existing selection—a small plus sign appears on the cursor. Use the Rectangular Marquee Tool (press M) to drag a selection around the bottom half of a shape, then Shift-drag around the top square to combine both areas into one selection. The Options Bar contains four selection icons: New Selection, Add To Selection, Subtract From Selection, and Intersect With Selection. By default, dragging a new selection replaces the previous one, but Shift temporarily activates Add To Selection, letting you build complex selections without losing earlier work.

Open the Paragraph panel via Window > Paragraph or the toggle icon in the Options Bar, then click its name tab. Use the top row of icons to align text (Left Align, Center, Right Align) or to justify it so every line fills the text box width. The four justification options—Justify Last Left, Justify Last Centered, Justify Last Right, and Justify All—differ only in how they handle the paragraph’s final line. Below the alignment row, adjust Indent Left Margin, Indent Right Margin, Indent First Line, and add Space Before or After Paragraph using the numeric fields.

Open the Character panel via Window > Character or the toggle icon in the Options Bar to access advanced type controls beyond the Options Bar. Set font family, style, size, and text color from either location—they are identical. Apply anti-aliasing (Sharp, Crisp, Strong, or Smooth) from the panel’s lower-right corner or the Options Bar to smooth letter edges; the default Sharp method works for most text, but test each to see subtle differences in edge transition.

Use the Type Tool (T) to click and drag out a rectangular text box, then release to create an area type container; text you type will automatically wrap to the next line when it reaches the box’s edge, unlike point type which requires manual line breaks via Enter/Return. The text box includes resizing handles similar to Free Transform, and you can hold Shift while dragging to force a perfect square. Font, style, size, color, and alignment options are set in the Options Bar before or after drawing the box.

Select the Type Tool from the Tools panel or press T, then click in your document to create point type. Choose a font, style, and size from the Options Bar; the font list shows a preview of each typeface, and you can adjust the preview size under Edit (Windows) or Photoshop (Mac) > Preferences > Type > Font Preview Size.

Combine two or more vector shapes on a single Shape layer using the Add to Shape Area, Subtract from Shape Area, Intersect Shape Areas, and Exclude Overlapping Shape Areas options in the Options Bar. Select a Shape tool, ensure Shape Layers mode is active, then click the desired icon before drawing the next shape. The vector mask thumbnail on the layer must have a white highlight border; if the options appear grayed out, click the thumbnail to select it. Add merges the new shape into the existing one, Subtract removes the overlapping portion, Intersect keeps only the overlap, and Exclude hides the overlap.

Choose the Shape Layers option in the Options Bar to draw resolution-independent vector shapes made of mathematical points, lines, and curves rather than pixels. Unlike pixel-based shapes, vector shapes remain crisp and sharp at any size and always print at the printer’s highest resolution. The Layers panel automatically adds a new Shape layer with a color swatch you set in the Options Bar, and Photoshop handles all the math when you resize or reshape.

Use the five geometric Shape tools—Rectangle, Rounded Rectangle, Ellipse, Polygon, and Line—to draw resolution-independent vector shapes by first selecting the Shape Layers option (the leftmost icon in the Options Bar) and choosing a color from the Color Picker via the color swatch. Switch between tools directly from the Options Bar icons after selecting one from the Tools panel. Drag to draw; release to fill the shape, and change its color later without quality loss.

Select the Move Tool to activate the Align and Distribute icons in the Options Bar, then Shift-click multiple layers in the Layers panel to enable them. Use Align Top Edges, Align Vertical Centers, or Align Bottom Edges to line up layer contents vertically, and Align Left Edges, Align Horizontal Centers, or Align Right Edges to line them up horizontally. For equal spacing between three or more layers, use Distribute Top Edges, Distribute Vertical Centers, Distribute Bottom Edges, Distribute Left Edges, Distribute Horizontal Centers, or Distribute Right Edges.

Organize your Layers panel by grouping related layers into virtual folders using layer groups. Click the folder icon at the bottom of the Layers panel to create a new empty group, or select multiple layers and choose Layer > Group Layers (Ctrl+G / Cmd+G) to bundle them instantly. Twirl the triangle icon to expand or collapse a group, revealing its indented child layers. Toggle the group’s own visibility eyeball to show or hide every layer inside at once. Nest groups within other groups for deeper hierarchy, and drag layers in or out of any group to reassign them.

Open Adobe Bridge, select multiple images using Shift-click, then go to Tools > Photoshop > Load Files into Photoshop Layers to open them all into one document, each on its own layer named after the original file. To view a single layer, Alt-click (Win) or Option-click (Mac) its eyeball icon in the Layers panel, which temporarily hides all others; click again to restore visibility. The document canvas automatically sizes to fit all images, so some layers may show transparency and require resizing with Free Transform. Rename layers by double-clicking the layer name and typing a new one, removing the file extension if desired.

The Background layer is a locked, special layer that Photoshop creates automatically when an image is opened. Unlike normal layers, its contents cannot be moved with the Move Tool—a lock icon in the Layers panel and a dialog box prevent it—and deleting pixels (for example, with Backspace/Delete after a Magic Wand selection) fills the area with the background color rather than transparency. To bypass these restrictions, convert the Background layer into a normal layer by renaming it or double-clicking its lock icon in the Layers panel.

Open any image in Photoshop CS5 and it lands on a locked Background layer, shown in italics in the Layers panel with a lock icon. Unlike normal layers, you cannot move its contents with the Move Tool—Photoshop warns the layer is locked—and deleting pixels with Backspace/Delete triggers the Fill dialog box instead of creating transparency because the Background layer does not support transparent pixels. To bypass these restrictions, convert the Background layer into a normal layer by renaming it or double-clicking its lock icon in the Layers panel, which unlocks full editing freedom.

Open an image in Photoshop CS5 and the Layers panel—found in the lower right or via Window > Layers—shows a single row for the Background layer, including its name, a preview thumbnail, and a lock icon. Click the New Layer icon at the bottom of the panel to add a new empty layer above it. The panel groups with Channels and Paths; click any name tab to switch between them. Each layer gets its own row, and the preview thumbnail shows the content on that specific layer.

Open the Layers panel, create a new white 800x600 pixel document, then use the Rectangular Marquee Tool to drag a selection and fill it with a color via Edit > Fill and the Color Picker, demonstrating how without layers every new shape or image element permanently alters the single Background layer, making it impossible to move, edit, or remove that red rectangle independently later.

Lower the Opacity value to 50% on a layer with layer styles, and the entire layer—including any applied effects like drop shadows, strokes, or bevel and emboss—fades uniformly. Lower the Fill value to 50% instead, and only the layer’s content (the pixels or text) becomes transparent, while the layer styles remain fully opaque. This distinction only matters when layer styles are present; on a plain layer without effects, Opacity and Fill behave identically. Both controls sit in the top-right corner of the Layers panel, with Fill directly below Opacity.

Use the Custom Shape Tool set to Paths mode to draw a shape outline, then fill it with text by clicking inside the path with the Type Tool. Choose any shape from the Shape Picker, draw it while holding Shift to maintain proportions, and reposition with the spacebar during the drag. After drawing, reshape or rotate the path with Edit > Free Transform Path (Ctrl+T / Command+T). Select a font, size, and color from the Options Bar, then click directly on the path outline to begin typing; the text will flow inside the shape’s boundaries.

Convert text into a shape via Layer > Type > Convert to Shape, then use the Direct Selection Tool (hidden behind the Path Selection Tool) to select anchor points around a letter’s interior and press Backspace/Delete to cut a hole. Switch to the Custom Shape Tool, choose a shape from the Options bar, and set the tool to “Subtract from Shape Area” to punch that shape out of the text. To add a shape, select “Add to Shape Area” instead. The shape layer’s vector mask thumbnail must be highlighted in the Layers panel before selecting or editing anchor points. Once text is converted to a shape, it is no longer editable as type.

Create repeating patterns from custom shapes by first opening a new 100x100 pixel transparent document, then adding vertical and horizontal guides from the rulers (View > Rulers) to mark the center. Select the Custom Shape Tool from behind the Rectangle Tool in the Tools panel, choose a shape from the Options bar, and draw it centered on the guides. Save the tile as a pattern via Edit > Define Pattern, then fill any layer with it using a Pattern Fill layer or the Paint Bucket Tool set to Pattern mode.

Add a solid color fill layer by clicking the New Fill or Adjustment Layer icon at the bottom of the Layers panel and choosing Solid Color, then pick a color in the Color Picker to replace the white background. To recolor the circles themselves, select the pattern layer first, then add another Solid Color fill layer above it; this will initially block the circles from view. Fix this by changing the fill layer’s blend mode from Normal to Multiply in the Layers panel, which lets the circles show through while applying the new color. For a gradient effect, use a Gradient fill layer instead of Solid Color, choosing a preset or custom gradient in the Gradient Fill dialog, and again set the blend mode to Multiply to color the circles while preserving their shape.

Create a 100×100 pixel transparent document, then add horizontal and vertical guides at 50% position (View > New Guide) to mark the exact center. Design your tile on this canvas, then save it as a pattern via Edit > Define Pattern. Apply the pattern by adding a new layer, opening the Layer Style dialog (Layer > Layer Style > Pattern Overlay), and selecting your saved pattern from the list. The tile size controls the repeat frequency—smaller tiles produce denser repeats. Guide colors can be changed in Preferences (Edit/Photoshop > Preferences > Guides, Grid & Slices) if the default cyan is hard to see.

Drag a photo from one tabbed document into another by first selecting the Move Tool, clicking inside the source image, and dragging its content onto the destination document’s name tab at the top of the window. Keep the mouse button held while hovering over the tab—Photoshop automatically switches to the target document—then drag downward into the canvas and release. This technique works around the tabbed interface introduced in CS4, which otherwise blocks the familiar drag-and-drop between floating windows. To revert to floating windows, disable Open Documents As Tabs in Edit/Photoshop > Preferences > Interface (Ctrl+K/Command+K).

Drag the Move Tool from one document window into another, holding Shift to center the image automatically. The Layers panel shows only the active document’s content, so click inside a window to make it active before dragging. For CS4/CS5 users, first go to Window > Arrange > Float All in Windows to avoid tabbed documents.

Draw type along any path in Photoshop using the Type Tool and a path created with Shape Tools, the Pen Tool, or custom shapes. Select the Ellipse Tool (or any shape tool), choose the Paths option in the Options Bar, then drag out your path. Switch to the Type Tool, pick your font and size in the Options Bar, and move the cursor directly over the path until it becomes an I-beam with a dotted wavy line. Click on the path and begin typing; the text follows the path's direction. After accepting the text with the checkmark, use the Path Selection Tool to click and drag the text along the path to reposition it, or drag across the path to flip it to the opposite side.

Connect a camera or memory card and use Adobe Bridge CS5’s Photo Downloader to select, preview, and transfer only the photos you want. First, enable the automatic launch of the Photo Downloader in Bridge’s Preferences (Ctrl+K / Command+K) under “When a Camera is Connected, Launch Adobe Photo Downloader.” Click the “Get Photos from Camera” icon or choose File > Get Photos from Camera, then switch from the Standard Dialog to the Advanced Dialog to see thumbnails of every image. From there, choose a destination folder on your hard drive, rename files if desired, and add author/copyright metadata before downloading—photos are stored in normal folders, not inside Bridge itself.

Hold down Shift while dragging any corner handle to resize proportionally, keeping the original shape intact without stretching or squashing the content. Use Ctrl+T (Win) / Command+T (Mac) to activate Free Transform, then drag side handles to adjust width or height independently, or hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) to reshape from the center outward. The bounding box appears around the active layer’s content, but Free Transform cannot select objects on its own—the content must already be on its own layer or selected beforehand.

Resize a selection outline without affecting the pixels inside using Photoshop's Transform Selection command, found under the Select menu or by right-clicking inside an active selection. Drag any corner or side handle to scale the outline; hold Shift while dragging to unlock the aspect ratio for independent width and height adjustments. Click and drag inside the transform box to reposition the outline, or drag just outside a corner handle to rotate it, with Shift snapping rotation to 15-degree increments. Unlike Free Transform (Edit > Free Transform), which resizes the selected pixels, Transform Selection modifies only the selection boundary itself.

Paint over the main subject with the Quick Selection Tool (shortcut W) to automatically select it based on tone, color, and texture, starting with a single click and then dragging like a brush. The tool defaults to "Add to selection" mode (shown by a plus sign in the cursor and the middle icon in the Options Bar), so you simply keep painting to expand the selection without holding Shift. If you accidentally select background areas, press Ctrl+Z (Win) / Command+Z (Mac) to undo the last stroke, or switch to the "Subtract from selection" mode in the Options Bar to paint over and remove unwanted regions.

Click the Magic Wand tool, nested behind the Quick Selection Tool in CS3 and later, then adjust the Tolerance value in the Options Bar to control how many shades lighter or darker than the clicked pixel are included in the selection. A Tolerance of 0 selects only the exact tone clicked, while the default 32 selects pixels up to 32 shades brighter or darker, making the tool ideal for selecting large areas of solid color but less effective on gradients or images with subtle tonal variations.

Set Photoshop CS5 to run faster and suit your workflow by adjusting key preferences. Open the dialog with Ctrl+K (Win) or Command+K (Mac), then set Image Interpolation to Bicubic Sharper for sharper results when reducing image size with the Image Size dialog, Crop Tool, or Free Transform. Disable Export Clipboard to prevent performance lags from copying large files to memory. Uncheck Use Shift Key For Tool Switch so pressing a letter key (like L for the Lasso, Polygonal Lasso, or Magnetic Lasso) cycles through nested tools without holding Shift.

Open Mini Bridge in Photoshop CS5 via the File > Browse in Mini Bridge command, the Window > Extensions > Mini Bridge menu, the Application Bar icon, or the collapsed panel icon. The panel displays a Home Page with Browse Files and Settings options; clicking Browse Files launches full Adobe Bridge in the background and reveals the Navigation Pod (top) and Content Pod (bottom). Navigate folders using Favorites, Recent Folders, Recent Files, or Collections headings in the left column, then preview and open images from the Content Pod without leaving Photoshop. Note that Mini Bridge does not support rating, labeling, or creating Collections—those require the full Bridge program.

Use Adobe Bridge CS4's Photo Downloader to transfer images from a camera or memory card to your computer. First, press Ctrl+K (Win) / Command+K (Mac) to open Bridge Preferences and enable "When a Camera is Connected, Launch Adobe Photo Downloader." Connect your camera or click the Get Photos from Camera icon to open the Photo Downloader, then switch to the Advanced Dialog format to see thumbnails with checkboxes, allowing you to deselect unwanted images before downloading. Bridge stores photos in normal folders on your hard drive, not within Bridge itself.

Launch Bridge CS4 from Photoshop's Application Bar or via File > Browse in Bridge (Ctrl+Alt+O / Command+Option+O) to open its three-column interface. Use the Back and Forward browse buttons to navigate through folder history, the down-pointing arrow to jump to parent folders or Favorites, and the Recent Files or Folders icon to revisit recently viewed files and folders. The central Content panel displays thumbnails of the currently selected folder, while the left and right columns hold panels for previewing, organizing, and adding metadata to images.

Paint with dynamically varying opacity and flow using the Other Dynamics section of the Brushes panel. Opacity controls the translucency of the entire brush stroke, remaining constant even where brush tips overlap; only a new, separate stroke can darken those areas. Flow, by contrast, controls the opacity of each individual brush tip, so overlapping tips within a single stroke combine to become more opaque. Access these controls normally from the Options Bar, but Other Dynamics lets you link them to pen pressure, stylus wheel, or other inputs via the Control dropdown, enabling strokes that fade or build up naturally as you paint.

Control hue, saturation, brightness, and purity of brush strokes using the Color Dynamics section in the Brushes panel. Set a Foreground and Background color, then use the Foreground/Background Jitter slider to randomly swap between them as you paint. Adjust the Hue Jitter, Saturation Jitter, and Brightness Jitter sliders to let Photoshop randomly vary those color attributes with each dab. Note that only the Foreground/Background option has a Control dropdown for pen pressure or fade; the Hue, Saturation, Brightness, and Purity sliders rely solely on jitter for randomization.

Mix two different brushes together using Photoshop’s Dual Brush options in the Brushes panel. Click directly on the words Dual Brush on the left side of the panel, then choose a second brush tip from the thumbnails on the right. Adjust the second brush’s Diameter and Spacing sliders independently from the main brush, and use the Scatter and Count sliders to spread multiple copies of the second brush inside the first. The initial brush acts like a cookie cutter—the second brush never extends beyond the first brush’s shape, no matter how large you make it.

Click directly on the word **Texture** in the Brushes panel to access options that let you paint with a pattern, simulating textured surfaces like canvas or paper. Choose a pattern from the Pattern Picker, loading additional sets like Artistic Surfaces via the fly-out menu and clicking **Append** to keep existing patterns. The **Mode** drop-down controls how the brush and texture blend, while the **Depth** options, initially grayed out, determine the texture’s visibility and are enabled by adjusting the **Depth Jitter** or **Control** settings. The **Texture Each Tip** checkbox applies the texture to every brush mark rather than the entire stroke, and the **Scale** slider resizes the pattern relative to the brush tip.

Scatter multiple copies of your brush tip along each stroke by opening the Brushes panel and clicking directly on the word Scattering (not its checkbox) to access the controls. Drag the Scatter slider right to spread brush tips apart, or enable Both Axes to scatter them randomly in all directions for a sprayed-on look. Use the Count slider to add extra brush tips per spacing interval, and choose a Control option like Fade, Pen Pressure, or Pen Tilt to dynamically vary the scattering amount—just ensure the Scatter value is above zero first, or no effect will occur.

Click directly on the words **Shape Dynamics** in the Brushes panel (not the checkbox) to access controls for dynamically varying a brush’s **size**, **angle**, and **roundness** while painting. Each section has a **Control** dropdown—set to **Off** by default—that lets you choose how the variation behaves. For size, the **Fade** option (the only choice that does not require a pen tablet) gradually reduces brush thickness over a set number of **steps** (default 25), making each successive brush stamp smaller until the stroke disappears.

Create a custom pixel-based brush by designing a black shape on a white 200 x 200 pixel document, then go to Edit > Define Brush Preset. The brush tip is grayscale: black creates 100% visible areas, white becomes transparent, and gray yields partial opacity. Use the Brush Tool with a small round brush (like Hard Round 5 Pixels) to draw the shape, ensuring the Foreground color is black. After defining the brush, open the Brushes panel (Window > Brushes) to adjust dynamic options like Shape Dynamics or Scattering, which alter the brush’s behavior. Note that custom brushes are pixel-based, so resizing them much larger than their default size will make them soft and dull.

Press Caps Lock to switch the Magnetic Lasso Tool’s cursor to a circle with a crosshair, revealing the exact area Photoshop scans for edges based on color and brightness differences. Click once on an object’s edge to set a starting point, then drag loosely around the shape; the tool snaps the selection outline to detected edges automatically. Access it by clicking and holding the Lasso Tool in the Tools panel, or cycle through all three lasso tools by pressing Shift+L.

Select the Polygonal Lasso Tool from behind the standard Lasso Tool in the Tools panel (or cycle through the three lasso tools with Shift+L). Click around the object to place anchor points; Photoshop automatically connects each new point to the previous one with a straight line. Close the selection by clicking the initial point—a small circle appears on the cursor when you are close enough—or double-click anywhere to let Photoshop close it with a straight line back to the start. Unlike the Rectangular Marquee Tool, the Polygonal Lasso Tool handles angled perspective, making it ideal for selecting straight-sided shapes like a billboard shot from an angle.

Select an object with the Lasso Tool by clicking and dragging a freehand outline around it, then release the mouse button to close the selection—Photoshop draws a straight line between start and end points if they don’t meet. Press L to activate the tool, or Shift+L to cycle through the standard, Polygonal, and Magnetic Lasso Tools. The Lasso Tool itself is imprecise, so improve results by first making a rough selection, then using the Options bar’s Add to Selection and Subtract from Selection modes to refine the edges.

Select the Elliptical Marquee Tool from behind the Rectangular Marquee Tool in the Tools panel, or press M (or Shift+M depending on your Preferences) to switch between them. Click and drag to draw an oval or circular selection outline. To create a perfect circle, hold the Shift key while dragging; to draw from the center outward, hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac). Once the selection is active, you can fill it with white using Edit > Fill, or apply any other effect without damaging the original image. The selection remains editable until you deselect it with Ctrl+D (Win) / Cmd+D (Mac).

Select the Rectangular Marquee Tool from the top of the Tools panel or press M, then click and drag from one corner of a rectangular area to the other to create a selection outline. Hold the spacebar while dragging to reposition the selection on the fly without restarting. Once the marching ants appear, any edit—such as changing color via Image > Adjustments > Hue/Saturation—affects only the selected pixels. The tool works for any rectangle or square, and the selection can be moved or adjusted before releasing the mouse button.

Selections bridge the gap between how humans and Photoshop see an image. While you recognize objects like apples, Photoshop only sees a grid of colored pixels. To edit a specific area—like changing an apple's color with the Brush Tool—you must first use a selection tool (Rectangular Marquee, Lasso, Magic Wand, or Color Range) to tell Photoshop exactly which pixels to target. Without a selection, any edit affects the entire image. The key caveat is that Photoshop cannot identify objects on its own; you must manually define the pixel boundaries for every adjustment, whether painting, filtering, or applying corrections.

Use the Arrange Documents icon in the Application Bar to quickly consolidate all open images into tabbed document windows or choose from new multi-document layouts that tile 2 to 6 images on screen. The menu’s second section offers layout icons—such as 2 Up vertical or horizontal, and 3 Up options—each showing a preview of the arrangement; grayed-out icons become available as you open more images. Tool Tips reveal the exact number of images a layout supports when you hover over an icon.

Open multiple images at once using File > Open with Shift-click to select them all, and they appear nested in a single document with each image’s name shown in a tab above the canvas. Click any tab to switch images, drag tabs left or right to reorder them, or press Ctrl+~ / Command+~ to cycle forward and Ctrl+Shift+~ / Command+Shift+~ to cycle backward (Ctrl+Tab / Control+Tab also works). If too many tabs are open, a double-arrow icon appears to the right, offering a fly-out menu to select any image. To float an image in its own window, drag its tab down and away from the others, or choose Window > Arrange > Float in Window. Note that keyboard shortcuts cycle images in the order they were opened, not the current tab order.

Navigate Photoshop CS4’s streamlined interface by identifying the Menu Bar at the top, the Tools panel along the left, and the Options Bar below them. The Tools panel defaults to a single column but can be toggled to a double-column layout by clicking the double-arrow icon at its top. Click and hold any tool with a small arrow at its bottom-right corner to reveal hidden, related tools from a fly-out menu. Note that the Mac version includes a Photoshop menu for Preferences, while Windows places Preferences under the Edit menu, and the Analysis and 3D menu headings appear only in the Extended version.

Save any combination of layer styles as a custom preset using the Layer Style dialog box's "New Style" button or the Styles panel's "Create New Style" icon. Apply a saved style instantly by clicking its thumbnail in the Styles panel, which loads all effects—drop shadows, strokes, glows, bevels, and more—onto the active layer. To share or back up presets, use the Styles panel flyout menu's "Save Styles" command, which writes a .asl file; load them later via "Load Styles" in the same menu. Note that Background layers cannot receive layer styles, so duplicate the layer first with Ctrl+J/Command+J. The stroke's default color is red, so change it via the color swatch in the Layer Style dialog, and set Position to Inside for a border that stays within the layer's edges.

Save only entire action sets, not individual actions, using the Actions panel menu’s Save Actions command, which writes a .atn file. Reset the panel to defaults with Reset Actions, then reload your saved set via Load Actions; the set reopens exactly as it was when saved.

Record a "Soft Glow" effect as a reusable Photoshop action using the Actions palette. Begin by clicking the New Action Set icon (folder icon) at the palette's bottom to create a custom set like "My Actions", then click the New Action icon to open the New Action dialog box. Name the action, assign an optional Function Key shortcut, and ensure it is placed inside your custom set rather than a default set. Once recording starts, perform the effect's steps—Photoshop captures every menu selection, tool use, and panel adjustment. After stopping recording, the action appears in your set and can instantly apply the soft glow to any image.

Create a new action set by clicking the New Action Set icon (folder icon) at the bottom of the Actions panel, then name it. Hold Alt (Win) / Option (Mac) and drag the Photo Corners action from the Frames set into your new set to copy it, leaving the original untouched. Double-click directly on the action’s name to rename it, then press Enter/Return. Always edit a copy rather than the original, and place it in a custom set instead of Photoshop’s default sets.

Hold down Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) and double-click any step in the Actions panel to play only that single step, advancing the highlight to the next step so you can watch exactly how an action builds its effect on your image and Layers panel. This technique reveals, for example, that the Photo Corners action begins by taking a snapshot stored in the History panel, then converts the image to RGB color mode—a step you can skip if your image is already RGB.

Open the Actions palette menu (click the menu icon in the top-right corner of the palette) and load any of the built-in action sets listed at the bottom—Commands, Frames, Image Effects, Text Effects, and more. Once loaded, the set appears below the Default Actions; click the triangle to expand it and see individual actions like Sepia Toning (layer). Select an action and click the Play icon at the bottom of the palette to run it automatically. Note that some actions in these sets duplicate those in the Default Actions set, and an empty dialog toggle icon means the action will run without any prompts.

Open the Actions panel (Window > Actions) and twirl open the Default Actions set by clicking its triangle. To run the Vignette (selection) action, first make a selection around the area you want to keep prominent, then select the action name and click the Play button at the bottom of the panel. Photoshop automatically applies a soft, dark border outside the selection. In Photoshop CS2, the classic default actions are replaced with workspace switchers; load the Sample Actions set from the panel’s fly-out menu to access the same Vignette and other standard actions found in other versions.

Open the Actions panel (Window > Actions) and play any pre-installed action by selecting it and clicking the triangular Play button. Record your own actions by clicking the New Action icon, performing steps like resizing or adding a watermark, then clicking Stop. Edit recorded steps by double-clicking a command to change its settings, or toggle dialog on/off to pause during playback. Save actions as .atn files via the panel menu to share or reload later.

Open the Edit menu, choose Keyboard Shortcuts, and in the dialog box select Application Menus from the Shortcuts For drop-down. Scroll through the list, twirl open Filter, then Blur, and click Gaussian Blur. Type your desired key combination directly into the Shortcut column—for example, Ctrl+Shift+G—then click Accept. Repeat for Unsharp Mask under Filter > Sharpen. Click OK to save the changes to the Photoshop Defaults set. Any shortcut you assign here instantly overrides the built-in shortcuts, so watch for conflicts flagged in red.

Fake a layer mask in Photoshop Elements by borrowing the built-in mask from an adjustment layer. Open two photos, drag one onto the other with the Move Tool (V), holding Shift to center it. Select the Background layer, then click the New Adjustment Layer icon at the top of the Layers palette and choose Levels (any type works). The adjustment layer appears between the two image layers with its own white layer mask. Alt-click (Option-click on Mac) the mask thumbnail in the Layers palette to view and edit it directly, painting with black to hide and white to reveal the top photo. This technique works because Elements supports layer masks only on adjustment layers, not on normal layers.

Press F7 to toggle the Layers panel open and closed. Create a new layer with Shift+Ctrl+N (Win) / Shift+Command+N (Mac) to open the New Layer dialog, or bypass it with Shift+Ctrl+Alt+N (Win) / Shift+Command+Option+N (Mac). Hold Ctrl (Win) / Command (Mac) and click the New Layer icon to add a layer below the current one, though this fails with the Background layer. Duplicate a layer or selection to a new layer with Ctrl+J (Win) / Command+J (Mac), or add Ctrl+Alt (Win) / Command+Option (Mac) to access the dialog. In CS2+, select all layers except Background with Ctrl+Alt+A (Win) / Command+Option+A (Mac), or right-click a layer and choose Select Similar Layers to grab all of the same type. Select contiguous layers by Shift-clicking the top and bottom, or use Shift+Alt (Win) / Shift+Option (Mac) with bracket keys; select non-contiguous layers by Ctrl-clicking (Win) / Command-clicking (Mac) each one.

Open the Preset Manager from the Edit menu, set the Preset Type to Custom Shapes, then click Save Set to save a group of selected shapes as a .csh file. Load a saved set later by clicking Load in the Preset Manager or choosing Load Shapes from the Custom Shape Tool’s shape picker fly-out menu. To organize shapes, Shift-click or Ctrl-click to select multiple thumbnails before saving; the Preset Manager also lets you rename or delete individual shapes. Remember that custom shapes always appear black in the picker regardless of their document color, and saving sets protects against losing shapes in a crash.

Draw custom shapes in Photoshop by tracing any object with the Pen Tool, then save the resulting path as a reusable custom shape. Select the Pen Tool and ensure the "Shape layers" icon is active in the Options Bar—this fills your path with color as you draw, letting you see the shape take form. Click to place anchor points and drag direction handles to create curved segments around your subject. Once the path is closed, go to Edit > Define Custom Shape, name it, and it appears in the Custom Shape Picker for instant reuse. A key caveat: the Pen Tool is essential for anything beyond basic rectangles or ellipses, and tracing works identically to freehand drawing, so no artistic skill is required.

Draw precise, editable selections using the Pen Tool by first creating anchor points and path segments that define the shape, then converting the completed path into a selection via the Paths panel's "Load path as a selection" button or the Ctrl+Enter (Win) / Cmd+Return (Mac) shortcut. Unlike pixel-based selection tools, the Pen Tool draws vector paths that remain independent of the image—they won't print or appear in saved JPEGs—allowing you to fine-tune curves and straight lines with the Direct Selection Tool (black arrow) before finalizing the selection. The Pen Tool lives in the Tools palette grouped with the Shape and Type tools, not with the Marquee or Lasso tools, because it creates paths rather than pixel selections. Mastering the Pen Tool requires practice, but once learned, the same Bezier-curve logic applies across most graphics and layout programs.

Open the image you want to sample from, delete all default swatches by Alt/Option-clicking each one in the Swatches panel (the cursor becomes scissors), then select the Eyedropper Tool (I) and click a color to sample it into the Foreground swatch. Click the empty Swatches panel area with the paint bucket cursor, name the swatch in the dialog, and repeat for each color. Save the set via the Swatches panel menu (Save Swatches), and later reload it using Load Swatches or reset defaults with Reset Swatches.

Add a layer mask to any layer by clicking the Add Layer Mask icon at the bottom of the Layers panel, then paint with black on the mask to hide areas and white to reveal them, using the Brush Tool for precise control. Unlike the Opacity slider, which fades an entire layer uniformly, a layer mask lets you create partial transparency in specific regions, enabling seamless blends like fading one photo into another. The mask itself stores transparency data as a grayscale image—black conceals, white reveals, and gray gives partial transparency—so you can edit it non-destructively at any time without altering the original pixels.

Open Photoshop CS3’s Preferences via Edit (Win) or Photoshop (Mac) menu, or Ctrl+K / Command+K, then set Image Interpolation to Bicubic Sharper for best results when reducing image size, cropping, or transforming. Disable Export Clipboard to avoid slowdowns and error messages unless you regularly paste Photoshop content into other programs. Optionally, turn off Automatically Launch Bridge if you rarely use it, and adjust Use Shift Key For Tool Switch to control whether pressing Shift cycles through grouped tools.

Work through ten layers tutorials organized in a logical sequence, starting with the Layers panel basics and moving through layer types, opacity, blend modes, layer masks, adjustment layers, type layers, layer styles, Smart Objects, and layer comps. Each tutorial builds on the previous one, so you progressively master adding, deleting, reordering, grouping, and merging layers, plus non-destructive editing with masks and Smart Objects. The guide includes links to download print-ready PDFs for each tutorial.
